growth_records.cite: Madin JS et al. A synthesis of bacterial and archaeal phenotypic trait data. Sci Data 7:170 (2020); source dataset: silva.
growth_records.notes: growth_rate_per_h computed as ln(2)/doubling_time_h. Species-level trait record; culture mode/pH often unspecified; medium + uptake/secretion pending literature curation.

growth_records.strain: BW-RI K-12 derivatives (ryhB reporter strains, e.g. ZZS21/ZZS22/ZZS23/ZZS41/ZZS43)
growth_records.cite: Levine et al. Quantitative Characteristics of Gene Regulation by Small RNA. PLoS Biology (2007). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.0050229. [PMC1994261]
growth_records.snippet: cells were grown overnight in minimal media with antibiotics... To determine the growth rate, OD600 was measured every 60 min... the cell doubling rate (μ) was obtained as the slope of a linear fit of log2(OD600) versus
growth_records.notes: Doubling time reported directly as an approximate value (~2 h) for most of the RyhB/RNA-OUT reporter strains grown at 37°C in M63 + 0.5% glucose minimal medium (12-well plate, shaking, aerobic, batch culture until OD600=0.3). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/td = ln(2)/2 = 0.3466 h^-1. Value is an approximation across strains, not a single precise per-strain measurement; pH not stated.

growth_records.cite: Campos et al. Genomewide phenotypic analysis of growth, cell morphogenesis, and cell cycle events in Escherichia coli. Molecular Systems Biology (2018). DOI:10.15252/msb.20177573. [PMC6018989]
growth_records.snippet: the WT and ftsA* strains have a similar doubling time (72.3 ± 2.2 min versus 69.0 ± 3.2 min, mean ± standard deviation, n = 4)
growth_records.notes: Doubling time directly reported as 72.3 ± 2.2 min (mean±SD, n=4) for WT (BW25113) grown in M9+0.2% glucose+0.1% casamino acids at 30°C (per Methods). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/(72.3/60 h) = 0.575 h-1.

growth_records.cite: Campos et al. Genomewide phenotypic analysis of growth, cell morphogenesis, and cell cycle events in Escherichia coli. Molecular Systems Biology (2018). DOI:10.15252/msb.20177573. [PMC6018989]
growth_records.snippet: the WT and ftsA* strains have a similar doubling time (72.3 ± 2.2 min versus 69.0 ± 3.2 min, mean ± standard deviation, n = 4)
growth_records.notes: Doubling time directly reported as 69.0 ± 3.2 min (mean±SD, n=4) for ftsA* mutant strain WM1659 grown in M9+0.2% glucose+0.1% casamino acids at 30°C (per Methods, same conditions as WT). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/(69.0/60 h) = 0.603 h-1.

growth_records.medium: glucose minimal medium (chemostat), as reported for E. coli K-12 MG1655 by Taymaz-Nikerel et al. 2010 and used/recalculated in this paper

up.method: reported (linear regression of E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat glucose-uptake-rate vs. growth-rate data)

growth_records.cite: Orth et al. A comprehensive genome-scale reconstruction of Escherichia coli metabolism—2011. Molecular Systems Biology (2011). DOI:10.1038/msb.2011.65. [PMC3261703]
growth_records.snippet: Growth-associated and non-growth-associated maintenances values were recalculated based on recent E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat data for growth on glucose minimal media (Taymaz-Nikerel et al, 2010)... The NGAM of 3.15 mm
growth_records.notes: This paper explicitly reports (for iJO1366 model parameterization) that E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat data on glucose minimal medium (from Taymaz-Nikerel et al. 2010) gave a linear regression of glucose uptake rate (mmol gDW-1 h-1) vs. growth rate mu (h-1) with slope = 10.83 mmol glucose gDW-1 h-1 per unit mu and intercept = 0.17 mmol glucose gDW-1 h-1 (uptake rate at mu=0, i.e., pure maintenance uptake); NGAM = 3.15 mmol ATP gDW-1 h-1 (=intercept x number of ATP per glucose at P/O=1.375) and GAM = 53.95 mmol ATP gDW-1 were derived by FBA from this same regression. No single discrete growth rate/dilution rate value or explicit medium recipe/temperature/pH/aerobic-anaerobic status is given in this paper's own text for the underlying chemostat experiments (only the fitted regression parameters are reported here), so growth_rate_per_h/doubling_time_h/temperature/pH/oxygen are left null; only the explicitly-stated regression relationship for glucose uptake is captured as an uptake_rate. No product secretion rate is reported/calculable from the numbers given in this paper. [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature]

growth_records.cite: Chen et al. Population Fitness and the Regulation of Escherichia coli Genes by Bacterial Viruses. PLoS Biology (2005). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.0030229. [PMC1151598]
growth_records.snippet: There is a dramatic increase in doubling time when these strains are grown on succinate as the only carbon source (τ = 139 min vs. 278 min), and very little difference on glucose.
growth_records.notes: Doubling time (139 min) directly reported in text for DH5αPro(pcI) grown in M9 minimal + 0.4% succinate. Growth rate calculated as mu = ln2/doubling_time = 0.693/(139/60 h) = 0.693/2.3167 h = 0.2992 h^-1. Medium: M9 minimal media (Sambrook and Russell) supplemented with 1 µg/mL vitamin B12, 0.4% succinate as sole carbon source (Materials and Methods, 'Bacterial doubling times').

growth_records.snippet: E. coli W3350 and W3350(λ) were grown in Tryptone medium with aeration, and 12 samples from each culture were taken about every 6 min between OD600 0.1 and 0.6.
growth_records.notes: Approximate exponential growth rate calculated assuming the stated OD600 range (0.1 to 0.6) was spanned by the described sampling scheme (12 samples taken every ~6 min, i.e. an elapsed window of (12-1)x6 = 66 min = 1.1 h). mu = ln(OD_final/OD_initial)/time = ln(0.6/0.1)/1.1 h = ln(6)/1.1 = 1.7918/1.1 = 1.63 h^-1; doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.425 h (25.5 min). Temperature not stated in text (left null). This is an approximation from indirectly-linked numbers (OD range and sampling cadence were not explicitly stated to correspond 1:1), hence low confidence; applies to both W3350 and W3350(λ), which were grown/sampled in parallel for the microarray time course and not reported as differing significantly in this rich-medium condition.

growth_records.snippet: These have been evolved since 1988 in 50 mL flasks containing 10 mL of DM media with 139 µM glucose (25 mg/L) as a carbon source. The cultures were grown at 37°C while shaking at 120 rpm, and were transferred daily via 1
growth_records.notes: Calculated from the paper's explicitly stated serial-transfer regime: 1:100 daily dilution corresponds to log2(100)=6.64 doublings/day (paper states this figure directly as '~6.64 net doublings per day'). doubling_time_h = 24 h / 6.64 = 3.61 h; growth_rate_per_h (mu) = ln(2)/3.61 = 0.192 h-1. This represents the average NET growth rate over the full 24-h daily serial-transfer cycle (lag + exponential + glucose-exhausted stationary phase), not a continuous single-phase exponential rate, so it is a lower-bound/average approximation of true exponential mu during the glucose-replete portion of the cycle; flagged medium confidence for this caveat. No explicit numeric growth-rate (h-1) values were given elsewhere in the paper for the many strain/substrate combinations profiled in Figures 1-4 (only log-ratio heatmap figures), so those were not extracted.

growth_records.notes: Calculated average specific secretion rate qP = titer / (initial DCW x time). Inputs from text: final acetone titer 17.46 g/L; initial OD600 = 18, converted to DCW using paper's stated factor 1 OD600 = 0.33 g/L DCW -> 5.94 gDCW/L; bioreactor run time 96 h. qP = 17.46 g/L / (5.94 gDCW/L x 96 h) = 0.03062 g/gDCW/h = 30.62 mg/gDCW/h; converted to molar using acetone MW 58.08 g/mol = 0.527 mmol/gDCW/h. Assumes approximately constant biomass over the run because this is a 'resting catalytic' (non-growing) stage per the paper's own iPBRC framework; this is an approximation since exact biomass time-course was not given, hence low confidence.

growth_records.strain: MG1655 (nominal serial-passage selection-cycle rate, all 88 evolving populations)
growth_records.cite: Oz et al. Strength of Selection Pressure Is an Important Parameter Contributing to the Complexity of Antibiotic Resistance Evolution. Molecular Biology and Evolution (2014). DOI:10.1093/molbev/msu191. [PMC4137714]
growth_records.snippet: Populations were grown for approximately 22 h and the populations surviving in the highest drug concentration were transferred to new culture vials (yielding 60× dilution, 6–7 generations per day if new mutants do not ap
growth_records.notes: CALCULATED nominal growth rate for the serial-transfer selection protocol used for all 88 evolving populations: 60-fold dilution every ~22 h implies mu = ln(60)/22h = 4.094/22 = 0.186 h^-1; doubling_time = ln2/mu = 3.73 h (paper itself states this equates to 6-7 generations/day, consistent with log2(60)=5.9 generations/22h). Low confidence: this is the protocol's designed/nominal rate assuming full growth back to the pre-dilution density each cycle in the presence of near-MIC antibiotic ('if new mutants do not appear'), not a directly measured single-strain growth rate, and is far slower than the drug-free doubling times (48-71 min) reported elsewhere in the same paper because cells here are growing under antibiotic stress near their MIC.

growth_records.notes: Approximate doubling_time_h CALCULATED from paper's stated duration/generation count: ~10 h for 10 generations => average doubling_time_h = 10/10 = 1.0 h; growth_rate_per_h = ln2/1.0 = 0.69 h-1. This is for E. coli BW25113 Δcas3-CRISPR1 ΔlacIZ grown in turbidostat mode (Chi.Bio platform, constant OD600 ~0.4) in M9CG medium at 37°C. Value is approximate (paper states 'at least 10 generations (~10 h)') rather than a precisely reported single growth-rate figure, hence medium confidence.

growth_records.snippet: in the presence of 10 nM PQQ, the strain grew to a final optical density (OD) of ~0.8, with a doubling rate of ~4 hours.
growth_records.notes: growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/4 h = 0.1733 h^-1, calculated from the reported doubling rate of '~4 hours' (approximate value, hence medium confidence). Strain is E. coli BW25113 ΔptsΔpqqU phosphotransferase-system-deficient, PqqU-deficient strain complemented with wild-type PqqU on an arabinose-inducible plasmid; growth requires PqqU-mediated PQQ import to activate the PQQ-dependent glucose dehydrogenase Gcd for growth on glucose. M63 medium composition and pH 7 from Materials and Methods. Temperature 37°C is from the general 'Bacterial strains and growth conditions' section (not explicitly restated for this specific complementation growth assay) so is a plausible but not 100% certain assumption; aerobic inferred from shaking batch culture (not explicitly labeled 'aerobic' in text).

growth_records.snippet: The values of rE, rS, and rC are based on empirically determined exponential growth rates for E. coli and S. Typhimurium in LB, which are approximately 0.033 min-1 for both these species. For a generation time of approxi
growth_records.notes: Paper states empirically determined exponential growth rate of 0.033 min-1 for E. coli in LB (used to parameterize a Lotka-Volterra invasion model); converted to per-hour: mu = 0.033 min-1 * 60 = 1.98 h-1. growth_records.medium: in vivo murine subcutaneous CT-26 tumor (intravenous injection colonization model); not a defined lab medium
growth_records.cite: Sayin et al. Bacterial population dynamics during colonization of solid tumors. Molecular Systems Biology (2025). DOI:10.1038/s44320-025-00175-5. [PMC12953616]
growth_records.snippet: If the most successful clone reaches a population size of 2.5 × 10^8 within a day of injection, it divides roughly 28 times during that period (corresponding to a generation time of ~50 min).
growth_records.notes: Calculated in-vivo growth estimate for the leading (largest) bacterial clone colonizing a tumor after intravenous injection. Inputs from paper: final population N=2.5×10^8 cells, starting from a single founder cell, elapsed time t=1 day=24 h, giving doublings=log2(2.5e8)=~27.9 (paper states 'roughly 28'), generation time = 24h/28 = 0.857 h (paper rounds to '~50 min' = 0.833 h, used here). growth_rate_per_h = ln2/0.833 = 0.832 h-1 (calculated). This is an in vivo estimate, not a defined-medium culture; temperature taken as normal mouse body temperature (37°C), not explicitly restated in the quoted sentence. Oxygen tension inside solid tumors is typically reduced/heterogeneous but not quantified in the paper.

growth_records.cite: Blattman et al. Prokaryotic Single-Cell RNA Sequencing by In Situ Combinatorial Indexing. Nature Microbiology (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41564-020-0729-6. [PMC8330242]
growth_records.snippet: For exponential cells, E. coli and S. aureus cultures were grown for approximately 2 hours until reaching an OD600 of 0.4... For stationary cells, E. coli MG1655 cells harboring pBbE2A-RFP were grown an additional 3 hour
growth_records.notes: Calculated mu = ln(OD_end/OD_start)/time using OD600 0.4 -> ~4 (average of Experiment 1.06 OD=4 and Experiment 1.10 OD=3.68, mean OD=3.84) over the stated 3 h interval: mu = ln(3.84/0.4)/3 = 0.755 /h; doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.92 h. CAVEAT: the paper itself describes the endpoint of this interval as reaching 'stationary' phase, meaning growth decelerated during the 3 h window (culture transitions from exponential to stationary), so this calculated mu is a lower-bound/average over a non-purely-exponential interval and likely underestimates the true mid-exponential specific growth rate. Grown at 37C with shaking (300 rpm, aerobic) per Methods; antibiotics (kanamycin 50ug/mL or carbenicillin 100ug/mL) present per plasmid selection, exact concentration used for this specific strain/experiment not disambiguated in text.

growth_records.snippet: We assumed that the amino acid limits the growth rate reaction only at very low concentrations...Therefore we fixed Kμ at a low value of 10-5 mM and set μmax to the measured growth rate on glucose of 0.6 h-1.
growth_records.notes: mu=0.6 h-1 stated as 'the measured growth rate on glucose' used to parameterize the kinetic model; paper's own cultivations were performed in M9 + 5 g/L glucose at 37C with shaking (methods section), consistent with this value, but the sentence does not explicitly re-state the strain/medium inline with the number, so provenance is inferred from the Methods section rather than directly co-located with the number. Doubling time calculated as ln2/0.6=1.155h (calculated by us from reported mu).

growth_records.notes: Doubling time and growth rate calculated, not directly reported, from the serial-transfer protocol: paper states a fixed daily 1:256 dilution yields 'eight generations of growth' in 24 h (2^8=256). doubling_time_h = 24 h / 8 generations = 3.0 h; growth_rate_per_h (mu) = ln(2)/3.0 = 0.231 /h. This reflects the designed propagation regime (applies to bulk population reaching the same dilution factor each day) in Davis minimal salts + 100 ug/L glucose (DM100), 37C, orbital-shaking batch culture; Davis minimal salts full composition not detailed in this paper (cites Lenski et al. 1991). Oxygen status inferred as aerobic from 'orbital shaking' incubation, not explicitly labeled in text.

growth_records.cite: Song et al. Kinetics-based inference of environment-dependent microbial interactions and their dynamic variation. mSystems (2024). DOI:10.1128/msystems.01305-23. [PMC11097648]
growth_records.snippet: TABLE 1 ... μ1max (1/h) 2.961×10−1 ... K1− (g/L) 3.091×10−4 ... YS−/X1 (g/OD) 1.372 ... YS1+/X1 (mg/OD) 1.550 (X1 = ΔtrpC E. coli mutant, glucose = S−, tryptophan = S1+)
growth_records.notes: mu1max=0.2961 h-1 is the maximal specific growth rate for X1 (ΔtrpC, JW1254-2) directly reported in Table 1 as a kinetic model parameter fit to batch/fed-batch experimental growth, glucose, and amino-acid time-course data (K3 minimal medium, glucose-limited, 37C, aerobic shake flask 225 rpm). doubling_time_h calculated = ln2/0.2961 = 2.34 h. Glucose uptake rate calculated as qS = mu * Y(S-/X1) = 0.2961 h-1 * 1.372 g/OD = 0.4064 g glucose/OD/h (paper's Y(S-/X1) is defined as g substrate consumed per unit OD of biomass, i.e. inverse of a conventional Yxs; units are OD-normalized, not gDW-normalized, because no OD-to-gDW conversion factor was given in this paper). Tryptophan secretion (cross-feeding product) calculated as qP = mu * Y(S1+/X1) = 0.2961 h-1 * 1.550 mg/OD = 0.459 mg/OD/h. Confidence medium: growth rate is a model-fit parameter (not a single raw time-course slope) and uptake/secretion units are OD- rather than gDW-normalized.

growth_records.snippet: TABLE 1 ... μ2max (1/h) 1.658×10−1 ... K2− (g/L) 3.923×10−4 ... YS−/X2 (g/OD) 1.773 ... YS2+/X2 (mg/OD) 2.994 (X2 = ΔtyrA E. coli mutant, glucose = S−, tyrosine = S2+)
growth_records.notes: mu2max=0.1658 h-1 is the maximal specific growth rate for X2 (ΔtyrA, JW2581-1) directly reported in Table 1 as a kinetic model parameter fit to batch/fed-batch experimental growth, glucose, and amino-acid time-course data (K3 minimal medium, glucose-limited, 37C, aerobic shake flask 225 rpm). doubling_time_h calculated = ln2/0.1658 = 4.18 h. Glucose uptake calculated as qS = mu * Y(S-/X2) = 0.1658 h-1 * 1.773 g/OD = 0.2940 g glucose/OD/h (OD-normalized, not gDW, since no OD-to-gDW factor given). Tyrosine secretion calculated as qP = mu * Y(S2+/X2) = 0.1658 h-1 * 2.994 mg/OD = 0.4963 mg/OD/h (value 2.994 for YS2+/X2 taken from a later in-text restatement of Table 1, 'determined to be 1.550 (=YS1+/X1) and 2.994 (=YS2+/X2) (mg/OD) through data fit'). Confidence medium for same reasons as the X1 record.

growth_records.snippet: Given a reactor volume of ~20 μl for the microfluidic device, the effective dilution rate (D) is thus ~2/h. This dilution rate is greater than the growth rate of MG1655 cells (< 1/h under our experimental conditions)
growth_records.notes: Authors state as fact (not a precise measured value) that MG1655 growth rate in their M9-based microfluidic experimental conditions at 37C is '<1/h'; this is an approximate qualitative upper bound given directly in the text, not a formula-derived calculation. No exact numeric growth rate, doubling time, or uptake/secretion rate was reported elsewhere in this paper. Dilution rate of ~2/h for the microfluidic device is explicitly stated (perfusion 4 µl/min for 5 min every 30 min into a ~20 µl reactor).

growth_records.cite: Wong et al. Mechanical strain-sensing implicated in cell shape recovery in Escherichia coli. Nature Microbiology (2017). DOI:10.1038/nmicrobiol.2017.115. [PMC5540194]
growth_records.snippet: We extracted the normalized growth rate λ from the arclengths...found a population-averaged value of ⟨λ⟩ = 0.021 min−1...This value of ⟨λ⟩ reflects a doubling time of td = 33 min, in agreement with bulk culture growth me
growth_records.notes: Paper reports doubling time td=33 min (0.55 h) and elongation rate λ=0.021 min-1 for filamentous SulA-induced E. coli MG1655 cells recovering from confinement (Phase 2, unconfined), stated to be in agreement with bulk LB culture growth measurements. growth_rate_per_h = ln2/doubling_time_h = 0.693/0.55 = 1.26 h-1 (calculated from the reported td). Growth medium is LB per Methods (used throughout except where M63 minimal media noted for fluorescence experiments); exact incubation temperature for this specific λ measurement is not restated at that point in the text, but 37°C is the temperature used for the corresponding SulA-filamentation/imaging protocol described in Methods, so recorded with medium confidence. This reflects elongation/growth rate of filamentous (division-inhibited) cells, not a conventional balanced-growth batch culture, which further limits confidence for GEM-constraint use.

up.method: reported Monod kinetics of mu_g vs acetate concentration; mu_max=0.34 h-1, Ks=0.5 g/L (Fig 3A)

growth_records.cite: Kotte et al. Phenotypic bistability in Escherichia coli's central carbon metabolism. Molecular Systems Biology (2014). DOI:10.15252/msb.20135022. [PMC4299493]
growth_records.snippet: Dependence of the growing subpopulation's growth rate, μg, on the acetate concentration is hyperbolic, with a maximal growth rate (μmax) of 0.34 h and Monod constant (KS) of 0.5 g l−1.
growth_records.notes: E. coli K-12 BW25113 wild-type. mu reported = mu_max 0.34 h-1 for the growing subpopulation on acetate (Monod fit, Fig 3A); doubling_time=ln2/0.34=2.04 h. Monod Ks=0.5 g/L acetate. Derivable mu_g at specific acetate conc via Monod mu=mu_max*S/(Ks+S): at 0.75 g/L => 0.34*0.75/1.25=0.204 h-1; at 2 g/L => 0.34*2/2.5=0.272 h-1. Growth rate refers only to the growing (adapting) subpopulation (fraction alpha), not the whole population. Batch M9 + acetate 0.75 or 2 g/L, 37°C, 300 rpm, pH~7. No qP/secretion reported.

growth_records.snippet: The steady-state fumarate uptake rates at the different induction levels 0, 1, 10, and 100 μM were 1.8, 2.1, 2.6, and 3.7 × 10−6 nmol cell−1 h−1 (see Supplementary Fig S6).

growth_records.cite: Biselli et al. Slower growth of Escherichia coli leads to longer survival in carbon starvation due to a decrease in the maintenance rate. Molecular Systems Biology (2020). DOI:10.15252/msb.20209478. [PMC7273699]
growth_records.snippet: As a control, wild-type cells (WT) are grown in batch culture in glycerol minimal medium (mu_WT = 0.70 h-1).
growth_records.notes: mu = 0.70 h-1 reported verbatim in text (Methods/Results, chemostat section). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.70 = 0.990 h (calculated). Medium = N-C- minimal (Csonka 1994) per liter K2SO4 1 g, K2HPO4.3H2O 17.7 g, KH2PO4 4.7 g, MgSO4.7H2O 0.1 g, NaCl 2.5 g; supplemented 20 mM NH4Cl (N source) + 5 mM glycerol (sole C source); 1 mM IPTG added only when needed to induce lac (not required for glycerol growth). No acetate excreted on glycerol at mu<=0.7 h-1 (stated). No specific substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported in this paper (it reports death rate, maintenance rate, recycling yield instead).

growth_records.cite: Biselli et al. Slower growth of Escherichia coli leads to longer survival in carbon starvation due to a decrease in the maintenance rate. Molecular Systems Biology (2020). DOI:10.15252/msb.20209478. [PMC7273699]
growth_records.snippet: we use a GlpK22 (NQ898) mutant without catabolic repression of the GlpK enzyme that grows 30% faster than wild type in batch culture in minimal medium supplemented with glycerol (mu_GlpK22 = 0.89 h-1).
growth_records.notes: mu = 0.89 h-1 reported verbatim (also written as 0.9 h-1 in Fig EV1). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.89 = 0.779 h (calculated). Same N-C- minimal + 20 mM NH4Cl + 5 mM glycerol medium as WT. Text states acetate excretion occurs at mu>0.7 h-1 (e.g. NQ898), so cells were washed/re-suspended in C-free medium before starvation; excretion is qualitative only, no flux value given.

Trace metal solution (per L): 0.48 g CuSO4.5H2O, 0.54 mg CoCl.7H2O, 0.54 g ZnSO4.7H2O, 2 g CaCl2.2H2O, 41.8 g FeCl3.6H2O, 0.3 g MnSO4.H2O, 33.4 g Na2EDTA, 0.5 g Na2MoO4.2H2O. growth_records.strain: B/r (reference for biomass composition, Neidhardt et al. 1990)
growth_records.cite: Feist et al. A genome-scale metabolic reconstruction for Escherichia coli K-12 MG1655 that accounts for 1260 ORFs and thermodynamic information. Molecular Systems Biology (2007). DOI:10.1038/msb4100155. [PMC1911197]
growth_records.snippet: we used the dry weight composition data for an average E. coli B/r cell growing exponentially at 37°C under aerobic conditions in glucose minimal medium, with an approximate doubling time of 40 min having a dry cell weight of 2.8 × 10 −13 g
growth_records.notes: Reference growth state used to define the iAF1260 biomass objective. Doubling time = 40 min = 0.667 h. mu = ln2/td = 0.693/0.667 = 1.04 h^-1 (calculated). Value cited from Neidhardt et al 1990; glucose minimal medium composition not specified in this paper.

up.method: model constraint; chosen to match maximum in-vivo O2 uptake (Edwards et al 2001; Fischer et al 2004)

growth_records.cite: Feist et al. A genome-scale metabolic reconstruction for Escherichia coli K-12 MG1655 that accounts for 1260 ORFs and thermodynamic information. Molecular Systems Biology (2007). DOI:10.1038/msb4100155. [PMC1911197]
growth_records.snippet: We found that an NGAM value of 8.39 mmol ATP g DW−1 h−1 and a GAM value of 59.81 mmol ATP g DW−1 best fit the experimental data... GUR of 10 mmol g DW−1 h−1, an OUR of 20 mmol g DW−1 h−1... maximum OUR set to 18.5 mmol g DW−1 h−1
growth_records.notes: These are model/derived values for E. coli K-12 MG1655 under aerobic glucose-limited conditions, NOT measured single-condition physiology. Maintenance fit to chemostat data: NGAM=8.39 mmol ATP/gDW/h, GAM=59.81 mmol ATP/gDW. FBA constraints used: glucose uptake (GUR) 10 mmol/gDW/h; O2 uptake (OUR) 20 mmol/gDW/h in gene-deletion sims and 18.5 mmol/gDW/h max in sensitivity analysis (18.5 stated to closely match maximum in-vivo O2 uptake, Edwards 2001/Fischer 2004). Model growth rate output not given numerically in main text; raw chemostat mu/GUR pairs are in the paper's Supplementary information (not in the read XML).

sec.units: uM (extracellular concentration, not a specific flux)
sec.method: reported HPLC; unstressed isolates and wildtype produced up to ~300 uM indole via tryptophanase (tnaA); under norfloxacin stress only the highly resistant isolate maintained production

growth_records.cite: Lee et al. Bacterial charity work leads to population-wide resistance. Nature (2010). DOI:10.1038/nature09354. [PMC2936489]
growth_records.snippet: In the bioreactor, E. coli were continuously cultured with a dilution rate of 0.48h-1... grown from a single colony, was diluted 1:10,000 in the bioreactor and grown, dilution rate of 0.48h-1, to OD600 ~ 0.6... these isolates were capable o
growth_records.notes: Continuous bioreactor at fixed dilution rate 0.48 /h; at steady state mu = D = 0.48 /h (calculated), doubling time = ln2/0.48 = 1.44 h. This is the operational growth rate before/at the antibiotic-selection setpoint (growth held to <=60% inhibition under drug). LB is a complex undefined medium; components not enumerated in paper (medium_composition_stated=false). Temperature 37C stated; pH not reported (unbuffered LB). No substrate uptake or product fluxes quantified. Indole is reported as an extracellular concentration (~300 uM) not a specific production rate; captured under secretion for completeness. Succinate noted qualitatively (astD upregulation) with no rate.

growth_records.strain: K-12 (motile HE206 / NCM3722 background and related MG1655B, RP437 strains)
growth_records.cite: Cremer et al. Chemotaxis as a navigation strategy to boost range expansion. Nature (2019). DOI:10.1038/s41586-019-1733-y. [PMC6883170]
growth_records.snippet: [Growth on serine is very slow (< 0.1/h) and not shown on the plot.]
growth_records.notes: Only numeric growth value present in the full text. Reported as an upper bound: batch-culture growth rate on serine as sole carbon source is <0.1/h. Derived doubling_time bound: td = ln2/mu; with mu<0.1/h => td > 0.693/0.1 = 6.93 h. No exact mu, no substrate/product fluxes are given in the article body; the paper's actual growth rates (all conditions: glycerol, glucose, acetate, mannose, CAA, +/- aspartate/serine) are tabulated in Supplementary Table 2 and aspartate uptake rates in Supplementary Table 7, neither of which is present in the provided XML, so no additional numeric records can be honestly extracted. Culture: modified MOPS minimal medium (Cayley et al.), trace metals omitted (inhibit motility); 37 C, 250 rpm, aerobic batch; growth rate measured as OD600 exponential slope.

growth_records.strain: NCM3722 (carrying plasmid pA5c-DOD-mCherry; P_LacUV5-driven DOPA dioxygenase DOD-mCherry, p15a ori, chloramphenicol resistance)
growth_records.cite: Mu et al. Exploring single-cell biosynthetic noise and dynamics for enhanced betaxanthin production in Escherichia coli. Nature Communications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-67733-1. [PMC12848102]
growth_records.snippet: the cell doubling time under our growth conditions (63 +/- 6 min). We cultivated the engineered betaxanthin-producing E. coli cells in microfluidic chambers under steady-state conditions, with a constant supply of defined minimal glucose me
growth_records.notes: Reported experimental single-cell doubling time td = 63 +/- 6 min = 1.05 h. Specific growth rate mu = ln2/td = 0.693/1.05 = 0.660 h^-1. Medium = modified M9 with 1% glucose + 0.1% Tween 80 + L-DOPA (microfluidics version); MOPS buffer pH 7.4 stated for the M9 recipe. Temperature: 37 C stated for LB preculture and plate-reader growth; microfluidic stage temperature not explicitly stated, assumed 37 C (all cultures in the study at 37 C). Host strain E. coli NCM3722 with pA5c-DOD-mCherry. Note: authors also grew 'Open-loop' cultures on alternative carbon sources (0.2% maltose, 20 mM fructose, 20 mM sorbitol, 20 mM arabinose) to generate different growth rates, but numeric mu values for those are only in Supplementary Fig. 13 (not in the XML) so no records emitted for them. Betaxanthin secretion is mentioned qualitatively (weak fluorescence in medium) but no secretion flux quantified; biomass yield appears only as a model parameter. No substrate uptake or product fluxes measured.

growth_records.cite: Nielsen et al. Multi-input CRISPR/Cas genetic circuits that interface host regulatory networks. Molecular Systems Biology (2014). DOI:10.15252/msb.20145735. [PMC4299604]
growth_records.snippet: 'After a ~90-min delay, fluorescence decreases concomitantly with cell dilution—occurring at a rate of 33 min per doubling.' (Fig 2F). Strains/media: E. coli K-12 MG1655* grown in MOPS EZ Rich Defined Medium (Teknova M2105) with 0.4% glycer
growth_records.notes: E. coli K-12 MG1655* in MOPS EZ Rich Defined Medium (Teknova M2105) + 0.4% glycerol, 37 C, 1000 rpm shaking (aerobic), batch. Reported doubling time 33 min = 33/60 = 0.55 h (Fig 2F, induction timecourse with 0.625 ng/ml aTc + 2 mM arabinose). mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/0.55 = 1.26 /h (growth_method=calculated from reported doubling). Full defined-medium salt/amino-acid composition is the commercial Neidhardt MOPS EZ Rich formulation and is NOT enumerated in the paper, so medium_composition_stated=false; only stated component is the 0.4% glycerol carbon source. Endpoint ODs also reported (OD600 0.44 with 0.625 ng/ml aTc dCas9 induction vs 0.51 uninduced after 6 h) but these are endpoints under 3-plasmid CRISPRi circuit induction, not clean growth rates, so not converted to mu. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates reported (this is a genetic-circuit study, not a flux study).

growth_records.cite: Iyer et al. Global Transcriptional Regulators Fine-Tune the Translational and Metabolic Efficiency for Optimal Growth of Escherichia coli. mSystems (2021). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00001-21. [PMC8546960]
growth_records.snippet: All characterizations were performed by growing cells in 400 ml M9 medium (6 g/liter anhydrous Na2HPO4, 3 g/liter KH2PO4, 1g/liter NH4Cl, 0.5 g/liter NaCl plus 2 mM MgSO4 plus 0.1 mM CaCl2) with 2 g/liter glucose ... temperature 37C, stirre
growth_records.notes: Medium and physiological conditions fully stated in Methods (Physiological characterization in a bioreactor). Growth rate, glucose uptake, ammonia uptake and mixed-acid product yields WERE measured (n=3) but the numeric values reside in Supplementary Table S1 (DOCX), which is not included in the provided XML. Main text gives only relative reductions (growth rate and glucose uptake reduced ~16-25%, P<0.05, in the three regulator-deletion mutants vs WT). No absolute rate could be extracted or computed from numbers explicitly present in the accessible text, so numeric fields are null. Conversion factor stated: OD600 of 1.0 corresponds to 0.44 g DCW/L.

up.units: net uptake maintained constant across [NH4+]ext; AmtB flux J_AmtB deduced but in normalized/relative units only (Fig 2E); onset of active transport at N*ext ~30 uM, internal maintenance ~15 uM
up.method: flux-balance deduction from growth rate and NH3 diffusion (J_AmtB = J_biomass + |J_diffusion|)

growth_records.cite: Kim et al. Need-based activation of ammonium uptake in Escherichia coli. Mol Syst Biol (2012). DOI:10.1038/msb.2012.46. [PMC3472687]
growth_records.snippet: MOPS-buffered minimal media (pH 7.4) ... For carbon sources, glycerol (0.4% w/v), glucose (0.4% w/v), or 10 mM each of glucose-6 phosphate and gluconate were used. For nitrogen sources, different concentrations of NH4Cl ... All cultures wer
growth_records.notes: E. coli K-12 NCM3722 grown in microfluidic 'chemostat' chambers at 37C, pH 7.4, glycerol 0.4% w/v as C source, NH4Cl titrated 4 uM-10 mM. Wild-type maintained a constant exponential growth rate from 10 mM down to a few uM NH4+; delta-amtB grew more slowly below ~20 uM. Numeric growth rates and ammonium fluxes (J_AmtB, J_diffusion, J_biomass) appear only in Figures 1E/2E as relative/deduced values; no absolute mu (h-1) or absolute mmol/gDW/h flux is stated in the text, so growth_rate_per_h left null. Km(GS)~100 uM; internal NH4+ maintenance ~15 uM; N*ext~30 uM. Full MOPS salt composition not spelled out (cited to Neidhardt 1974) -> medium_composition_stated:false.

up.units: AmtB transport activated at a higher external NH4+ and higher internal maintenance level than on glycerol (faster growth -> higher N demand); fluxes in relative units only (Fig 3)
up.method: flux-balance deduction from growth rate and NH3 diffusion

growth_records.notes: Same E. coli strains/device as glycerol record but glucose 0.4% w/v C source (faster growth, higher N demand -> AmtB activated at higher NH4+ and higher internal maintenance conc). Numeric mu and fluxes only in Figures 3A/3D as relative/deduced values; no absolute mu (h-1) or mmol/gDW/h in text -> growth_rate_per_h null. MOPS salt composition cited to Neidhardt 1974, not spelled out.

growth_records.cite: Kim et al. Need-based activation of ammonium uptake in Escherichia coli. Mol Syst Biol (2012). DOI:10.1038/msb.2012.46. [PMC3472687]
growth_records.snippet: 10 mM each of glucose-6 phosphate and gluconate were used ... successively higher maintenance concentrations for the faster growing cells, consistent with the higher internal NH4+ concentrations needed for assimilation by GS at the higher r
growth_records.notes: Third (fastest-growth) C-source condition: 10 mM glucose-6-phosphate + 10 mM gluconate, otherwise identical E. coli setup (37C, pH 7.4, NH4Cl titrated). Faster growth -> highest internal NH4+ maintenance conc and highest external NH4+ at which AmtB is activated. Numeric mu and fluxes only in figures as relative/deduced values; no absolute mu (h-1) or mmol/gDW/h stated -> growth_rate_per_h null. MOPS salt base cited to Neidhardt 1974.

growth_records.cite: Katz et al. Dynamics of Adaptation During Three Years of Evolution Under Long-Term Stationary Phase. Mol Biol Evol (2021). DOI:10.1093/molbev/msab067. [PMC8233507]
growth_records.snippet: Five independent LTSP populations were established, each by inoculating ~5 x 10^6 E. coli K12 strain MG1655 cells/ml of Luria broth (LB)... the populations initially grew to ~10^10 cells/ml, during the first day of the experiment.
growth_records.notes: Only paper of the four batch with an organism-level, computable growth. Explicit numbers: inoculum ~5e6 cells/ml; peak ~1e10 cells/ml reached 'during the first day' (~24 h). mu = ln(N/N0)/t = ln(1e10/5e6)/24 h = ln(2000)/24 = 7.601/24 = 0.317 /h. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.317 = 2.19 h. This is an approximate AVERAGE (lower-bound) rate because peak may have been reached in <24 h and includes lag; hence confidence low. 37C, 225 rpm, aerated flask, batch, aerobic all stated. LB composition not given in paper -> composition:[] , medium_composition_stated:false (not fabricated). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported.

growth_records.strain: K-12 MG1655 pCS-λ (luciferase reporter plasmid, kanamycin selection)
growth_records.cite: Held et al. Nutrient colimitation is a quantitative, dynamic property of microbial populations. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2024). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2400304121. [PMC11670248]
growth_records.snippet: The blue surface is a model fit to all replicate measurements (Poisson arrival time model with g_max ≈ 0.66 per hour, a_glu ≈ 23 per hour per mM glucose, a_amm ≈ 90 per hour per mM ammonium; R_glu,min ≈ 0.023 mM, R_amm,min ≈ 0.0039 mM).
growth_records.notes: gmax = 0.66/h is the saturating (max) per-capita growth rate fitted from luminescence growth curves of E. coli K-12 MG1655 pCS-λ across glucose(0-2 mM) x NH4Cl(0-1 mM) gradients in modified M9 at 37°C (Poisson arrival-time model; Materials and Methods, 'Measurement of Growth Rates from Luminescence Growth Curves'). Growth quantified by linear regression of log-luminescence over the exponential interval, hence growth_method=calculated. doubling_time = ln2/gmax = 0.693/0.66 = 1.05 h (calculated). Other fitted Monod-type parameters: glucose affinity a_glu ≈ 23 /h/mM, ammonium affinity a_amm ≈ 90 /h/mM, minimum resource concentrations R_glu,min ≈ 0.023 mM, R_amm,min ≈ 0.0039 mM — these are affinities, not uptake fluxes, so uptake_rates left empty (no mmol/gDW/h derivable). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes are reported. A SEPARATE growth-YIELD experiment (same paper) used E. coli K-12 MG1655 (chromosomal GFP) in a slightly different modified M9 base [Na2HPO4 6 g/L, KH2PO4 3 g/L, NaCl 0.5 g/L, MgSO4 1 mM, CaCl2 0.1 mM] with glucose(0-40 mM) x NH4Cl(0-40 mM), 37°C, 500 RPM shaking, 24 h, OD600 in Biotek LogPhase 600; it reports biomass yields only in arbitrary OD units (no gDW-based Yxs and no growth rate), so it is not emitted as a flux/rate record. pH not stated (phosphate-buffered M9, ~7). Reference conditions cited in text (not this strain's own growth): typical M9 = 0.2% glucose ≈ 11.1 mM glucose + 18.7 mM ammonium.

growth_records.cite: Pavlou et al. Single-cell data reveal heterogeneity of investment in ribosomes across a bacterial population. Nat Commun (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41467-024-55394-5. [PMC11695989]
growth_records.snippet: The mean growth rate over all mother cells considered in the experiment increases from 0.27 +/- 0.02 h-1 on acetate to 0.79 +/- 0.03 h-1 on glucose. The medium used for the microfluidics experiments was supplemented with 2 g/L of acetate or
growth_records.notes: mu reported = 0.79 +/- 0.03 h-1 (mean single-cell growth rate on glucose, mother machine). Doubling time computed td = ln2/mu = 0.693147/0.79 = 0.877 h (52.6 min). Growth rate estimated by the authors from single-cell length-elongation via Bayesian/Kalman inference; classified 'reported' since the paper gives the numeric value. M9 base salt concentrations are cited to ref.100 and not enumerated in the text; stated supplements: vitamin B1 5 mg/L, unspecified mineral trace elements, 2 g/L glucose. 37 C. Aerobic (continuous aerated medium flow). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported.

growth_records.snippet: All experiments were performed using M9 minimal medium, supplemented with 2 g/l glucose... inoculated to a starting OD600 of 0.03-0.05... incubated at 37C under shaking. Glucose and acetate concentrations in supernatants were determined by
growth_records.notes: Catabolic-limitation series of E. coli NCM3722-derived titration strains. Medium = M9 minimal + 2 g/l glucose, 37C, shaking (aerobic), batch (deep-well plates). Discrete per-condition growth rates (mu), specific glucose uptake and acetate secretion rates were measured (uptake/secretion by linear regression of extracellular metabolite vs OD600-derived CDW using 1 OD600 = 0.413 g CDW/l, and growth rate) across a continuum of inducer levels, but the numeric values are presented only in Appendix figures / Dataset EV (images / external xls) and are NOT present in the article XML text, so no single mu/qS/qP number could be transcribed. Medium composition rows for phosphate/sulfate/ammonium are taken from the explicitly stated modified/15N-reference M9 (42.2 mM Na2HPO4, 22 mM KH2PO4, 8.56 mM Na2SO4, 11.3 mM NH4Cl); trace elements/Mg were by reference (Kochanowski et al.) and not enumerated. No arithmetic performed (no explicit input numbers for rates).

yields.product_yields: acetate: up to ~0.5 of consumed glucose secreted as acetate under strong anabolic limitation (stated as 'up to 50% of the consumed glucose secreted as acetate'); reduced biomass yield consistent with N-limited chemostats

growth_records.cite: Kochanowski et al. Global coordination of metabolic pathways in Escherichia coli by active and passive regulation. Molecular Systems Biology (2021). DOI:10.15252/msb.202010064. [PMC8045939]
growth_records.snippet: Under anabolic limitation... acetate secretion occurred even at the lowest tested growth rate... we also observed substantial 2-oxoglutarate secretion... with up to 50% of the consumed glucose secreted as acetate and 2-oxoglutarate secretio
growth_records.notes: Anabolic-limitation series (GOGAT titration) of E. coli NCM3722 derivatives; M9 + 2 g/l glucose, 37C, shaking (aerobic), batch. Explicit qualitative/relative numbers in text: anabolic limitation caused up to fivefold growth-rate reduction (no absolute mu given, so mu left null); up to ~50% of consumed glucose secreted as acetate (product yield Y_ace/glc up to ~0.5, mol/mol on consumed glucose per the wording); besides acetate and 2-oxoglutarate, all other quantified metabolites were secreted at <70 umol/gCDW/h. Discrete per-level mu, qGlucose, qAcetate, qOxoglutarate values exist but only in Appendix figures/Dataset EV (images/xls) outside the XML text, so no absolute rates transcribed. No arithmetic performed (no explicit paired numerator/denominator numbers available in text).

growth_records.strain: SX43 (BW25993 background; Tsr-Venus fusion expressed from lacZ locus)
growth_records.cite: Windels et al. Antibiotic dose and nutrient availability differentially drive the evolution of antibiotic resistance and persistence. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae070. [PMC11102087]
growth_records.snippet: We set psi_max=0.6 h-1, which corresponds to an average of one division per cell per 30 min (at AB=0 ug/ml and n=1) for the ancestor ... in accordance with our experimentally measured killing rate ... psi_min=-10 h-1 ... at AB=400 ug/ml.
growth_records.notes: E. coli SX43 ancestor maximum growth. Paper states psi_max = 0.6 h-1, where psi is defined as change in log10(cell count) per hour, and 'corresponds to an average of one division per cell per 30 min' at zero antibiotic and full nutrient (n=1). Doubling time td = 30 min = 0.5 h (reported). mu (natural-log basis) = ln2/td = 0.6931/0.5 = 1.386 h-1 (calculated). Cross-check: mu = psi_max * ln(10) = 0.6 * 2.3026 = 1.382 h-1 (consistent). Killing rate psi_min = -10 h-1 (log10 basis) at 400 ug/ml amikacin corresponds to a drop of ~ -6 log10 units/h (biphasic first-phase kill of non-persisters); this is a death rate, not a growth/substrate flux, so not emitted as an uptake/secretion record. Value set as a model parameter but explicitly grounded in the authors' experimental time-kill/growth data. MHB is a complex undefined broth; composition not stated in paper (medium_composition_stated=false). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported.

sec.method: reported (slope of acetate vs OD600 x growth rate; exponential fit for OD600<0.3, td=18 min)
reported (alternative exponential fit for OD600 0.3-0.5, td=28 min)

growth_records.cite: Basan et al. Overflow metabolism in E. coli results from efficient proteome allocation. Nature (2015). DOI:10.1038/nature15765. [PMC4843128]
growth_records.snippet: Assuming exponential growth for OD600 data below 0.3 and alternatively from 0.3 for 0.5 gave doubling time of 18 min and 28 min, respectively. The corresponding acetate excretion rates were 14.3 and 3.6 mM/OD600/hr. These data should be reg
growth_records.notes: E. coli K-12 (NCM3722 parent strain, wild-type) grown on LB in well-shaken aerobic batch culture (Extended Data Fig. 1 note). Two exponential-fit estimates for the same LB culture: (1) OD600<0.3: doubling time td=18 min=0.30 h -> mu=ln2/td=0.6931/0.30=2.31 h-1 (calculated), acetate excretion 14.3 mM/OD600/h (reported); (2) OD600 0.3-0.5: td=28 min=0.4667 h -> mu=0.6931/0.4667=1.485 h-1 (calculated), acetate 3.6 mM/OD600/h (reported). growth_rate_per_h field carries the early-phase value (2.31 h-1); the late-phase alternative (1.485 h-1) is documented here. Both acetate rates emitted as separate secretion entries. Authors explicitly label these semi-quantitative because LB batch growth is non-steady. Temperature 37 C per the study's bioreactor/standard E. coli conditions (batch temperature not restated in text). LB (Miller) composition not stated in this paper (medium_composition_stated=false). Note: the paper's main quantitative dataset (Fig.1 acetate excretion vs growth rate across glycolytic carbon sources in MOPS minimal medium; bioreactor glucose uptake, O2, CO2 and acetate in Extended Data Fig.3a) resides in figure source-data/images and is not numerically present in the article text, so those per-condition uptake/secretion fluxes could not be transcribed without fabrication. Text-stated derived quantities not tied to a single culture (not emitted as records): acetate-line threshold growth rate lambda_ac ~= 0.76 h-1 (55 min/doubling), below which acetate excretion vanishes; proteome efficiencies epsilon_f ~= 750 and epsilon_r ~= 390 mM ATP/OD600/h. Minimal-medium base (MOPS-buffered, Cayley et al.): 40 mM MOPS, 4 mM tricine (pH 7.4 KOH), 0.1 M NaCl, 10 mM NH4Cl, 1.32 mM KH2PO4, 0.523 mM MgCl2, 0.276 mM Na2SO4, 0.1 mM FeSO4 + Neidhardt trace micronutrients; carbon-source concentrations were given only in figure tables (images), so no minimal-medium growth-rate record could be built.

growth_records.strain: MG1655 (gAM-513; chromosomal dCas9 at HK022 attB)
growth_records.cite: Mathis et al. A simplified strategy for titrating gene expression reveals new relationships between genotype, environment, and bacterial growth. Nucleic Acids Research (2021). DOI:10.1093/nar/gkaa1073. [PMC7797047]
growth_records.snippet: ...0.94 doublings h-1 in glucose...to rescale the time axis to units of generations
growth_records.notes: Paper reports the mixed (wildtype-like) E. coli MG1655 population growth in the turbidostat as 0.94 doublings h-1 in glucose. Converted to specific growth rate mu = doublings/h * ln2 = 0.94 * 0.6931 = 0.6515 h-1. Doubling time = 1/(doublings per h) = 1/0.94 = 1.064 h. Value is for the pooled CRISPRi library reference population (negC/wildtype-like); no substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported. Full M9 salt composition not given in text; only carbon source (0.4% glucose), kanamycin (35 ug/mL) and pH 6.5 stated. Population is aneuploid CRISPRi strain but WT-like at reference guide.

growth_records.snippet: ...(0.5 doublings h-1 in glycerol, 0.94 doublings h-1 in glucose) to rescale the time axis to units of generations (methods).
growth_records.notes: Paper reports the mixed (wildtype-like) E. coli MG1655 population growth in the turbidostat as 0.5 doublings h-1 in glycerol. Converted to specific growth rate mu = doublings/h * ln2 = 0.5 * 0.6931 = 0.3466 h-1. Doubling time = 1/0.5 = 2.0 h. No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported. Only carbon source (0.2% glycerol), kanamycin (35 ug/mL) and pH 6.5 stated; full M9 salt composition not given.

growth_records.snippet: conversion efficiencies for biomass with glucose of 0.30 ± 0.01 g cell dry weight [cdw] g glucose−1 for the controls ... The flow rate was adjusted manually as required to maintain a pseudoexponential growth rate of approximately 0.25 h−1
growth_records.notes: Control fed-batch (inactive CimA3.7dead). mu=0.25 h-1 reported (feed-controlled pseudoexponential). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.25 = 2.77 h. Yxs=0.30 g cdw/g glucose reported. qS glucose = mu/Yxs = 0.25/0.30 = 0.833 g glucose/gDW/h; /180.16 = 4.63 mmol/gDW/h. Medium components converted to final per-L: MgSO4·7H2O 2 ml of 246.47 g/L stock=0.493 g/L; salt solution 200 ml (×0.2): K2HPO4 73→14.6, NaH2PO4·2H2O 18→3.6, (NH4)2SO4 10→2.0, ammonium citrate 2.5→0.5 g/L; trace elements 2 ml (×0.002): Na2EDTA·2H2O 22.3→0.0446, FeCl3 10.03→0.0201, CaCl2·2H2O 0.5→0.001, ZnSO4·7H2O 0.18→0.00036, CoCl2·6H2O 0.18→0.00036, CuSO4·5H2O 0.16→0.00032, MnSO4·H2O 0.15→0.0003 g/L. Batch glucose 11.9 g/L. Control produced formate ~3 mM and acetate max ~1.7 mM (concentrations, not fluxes).

growth_records.snippet: 0.26 ± 0.01 g cdw g glucose−1 for the test fermentations ... accumulation of citramalate to 153 ± 5 mM in the test fermentations, which equates to 0.129 ± 0.003 mol citramalate per mol glucose consumed ... Acetate ... (maximally ∼1.1 mM) in
growth_records.notes: Test fed-batch (active CimA3.7, citramalate producer). mu=0.25 h-1 reported (feed-controlled pseudoexponential, same target as control). doubling_time=ln2/0.25=2.77 h. Yxs=0.26 g cdw/g glucose reported. qS glucose = mu/Yxs = 0.25/0.26 = 0.962 g/gDW/h; /180.16*1000 = 5.34 mmol/gDW/h. Citramalate yield 0.129 mol/mol glucose reported → qP citramalate = 0.129 * 5.34 = 0.689 mmol/gDW/h. Titer 153 mM = 25 g/L. Test also produced acetate max ~1.1 mM, no formate; citraconate ~3 mM extracellular. Medium conversion identical to control record (ML medium, see that record's notes).

growth_records.snippet: the absolute glucose uptake rate of dTCA-E1 strain (11.7 mmol/gDCW/h) was significantly higher than that of iTCA... The acetate yields of dTCA-E1 (0.82 +/- 0.06 mol/mol)... (mu = 0.61 +/- 0.02 h-1 for dTCA-E1).
growth_records.notes: Reported mu=0.61+/-0.02 /h; doubling_time = ln2/0.61 = 1.136 h. Reported glucose uptake = 11.7 mmol/gDCW/h. Acetate yield = 0.82+/-0.06 mol/mol glucose. growth_records.snippet: At 30°C, most TS mutants had similar specific growth rates as the control strain (0.35 h-1), whereas specific growth rates varied markedly at higher temperatures
growth_records.notes: Nonedited control E. coli BW25113, maximum specific growth rate mu=0.35 h-1 at 30°C on M9 minimal glucose (5 g/L glucose), reported (plate-reader growth). sec.method: reported; biomass-specific arginine production rate, constant during initial 6 h of growth arrest at 42°C, from linear regression of arginine (LC-MS/MS whole broth)

growth_records.cite: Schramm et al. Mapping temperature-sensitive mutations at a genome scale to engineer growth switches in Escherichia coli. Molecular Systems Biology (2023). DOI:10.15252/msb.202311596. [PMC10568205]
growth_records.snippet: During the initial 6 h of the growth arrest, the arginine production rate was constant at 1.35 mmol gDW-1 h-1, which is 59% of the production rate (2.3 mmol gDW-1 h-1) that can be achieved with the growing arginine production strain without
growth_records.notes: Engineered TS arginine-overproducing E. coli (delta-argR, ArgA-H15Y, ArgO overexpression, plus DnaX-L289Q growth switch) cultivated 24 h at 42°C on M9 glucose. Strain did NOT grow at 42°C (DnaX-L289Q TS mutation arrests growth), so mu=0 (reported growth arrest). Specific arginine secretion rate = 1.35 mmol/gDW/h (reported, constant over first 6 h). Reference value 2.3 mmol/gDW/h for the growing strain without DnaX mutation is from Sander et al. 2019 (cited, NOT measured here) and is not curated as a record.

growth_records.cite: Balagaddé et al. A synthetic Escherichia coli predator-prey ecosystem. Molecular Systems Biology (2008). DOI:10.1038/msb.2008.24. [PMC2387235]
growth_records.snippet: Conversely, the prey grew to a high density (∼35 000 cells per reactor) in ∼10 h ... precultures were then used to seed microchemostat reactors with ∼20 cells nl−1 ... dilution rate (D)=0.1125 h−1
growth_records.notes: Reactor volume ~9 nL; seeded at ~20 cells/nL => ~180 cells initial. Prey reached ~35,000 cells/reactor in ~10 h during initial growth phase. Apparent NET specific growth rate mu_net = ln(35000/180)/10 = ln(194.4)/10 = 5.270/10 = 0.527 h^-1. Because continuous dilution (D=0.1125 h^-1) removes cells during this phase, gross mu = mu_net + D = 0.527 + 0.1125 = 0.640 h^-1; doubling time = ln2/0.640 = 1.08 h. Estimate confounded by dilution and predation onset; approximate cell counts and time. No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported (LBK is a rich, undefined-carbon medium).

growth_records.cite: Balagaddé et al. A synthetic Escherichia coli predator-prey ecosystem. Molecular Systems Biology (2008). DOI:10.1038/msb.2008.24. [PMC2387235]
growth_records.snippet: The high prey density led to rescue of the predator population, which recovered from ∼5000 cells per reactor to >20 000 cells per reactor in ∼10 h ... dilution rate (D)=0.1125 h−1
growth_records.notes: Predator recovered from ~5,000 to >20,000 cells/reactor in ~10 h (rescue phase). Apparent NET mu = ln(20000/5000)/10 = ln(4)/10 = 1.386/10 = 0.1386 h^-1. Correcting for dilution: gross mu = 0.1386 + D(0.1125) = 0.251 h^-1; doubling time = ln2/0.251 = 2.76 h. Heavily confounded: this is a CcdA-antidote rescue recovery, not steady exponential growth; counts/time approximate. No metabolic flux data reported.

growth_records.cite: Balagaddé et al. A synthetic Escherichia coli predator-prey ecosystem. Molecular Systems Biology (2008). DOI:10.1038/msb.2008.24. [PMC2387235]
growth_records.snippet: Figure 3A illustrates coexistence dynamics at D=0.11 h−1 ... an increase in D (to 0.2 h−1) led to a phase of damped oscillatory dynamics with much shorter periods (∼50 h).
growth_records.notes: Continuous co-culture operated at dilution rates D=0.11 and 0.2 h^-1. Populations are oscillatory (not at simple single-species steady state), so mu is NOT equal to D and no specific growth rate is reported; dilution_rate captured as an operational condition. IPTG varied 0-50 µM across runs (oscillation elicited at IPTG >=5 µM). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported.

growth_records.cite: Balagaddé et al. A synthetic Escherichia coli predator-prey ecosystem. Molecular Systems Biology (2008). DOI:10.1038/msb.2008.24. [PMC2387235]
growth_records.snippet: These populations initially coexist at a low dilution rate (D=0.15 h−1). They switched to damped oscillations with short periods (∼30 h) upon an increase in D (to 0.23 h−1). Further increase in D (to 0.31 h−1) at ∼250 h led to predator wash
growth_records.notes: Continuous co-culture at dilution rates D=0.15, 0.23, 0.31 h^-1. Predator washed out at D=0.31 h^-1 (implies max predator specific growth rate < 0.31 h^-1 under these conditions, an upper bound only). Oscillatory dynamics => mu != D; no measured specific growth rate. No metabolic flux data reported.

growth_records.cite: Schreck et al. Impact of crowding on the diversity of expanding populations. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2023). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2208361120. [PMC10089160]
growth_records.snippet: Colonies starting from single cells were grown on plates with LB and 2% agar at 37 °C for 30 h up to a population size between 10^8 and 5·10^8 cells.
growth_records.notes: Paper reports NO explicit growth rate value for E. coli (only 'consistent with previously reported values'). Population-average specific growth rate derived from the two explicit endpoints: start N0=1 cell, final N=10^8 to 5x10^8 cells, t=30 h. mu=ln(N/N0)/t. Lower bound: ln(1e8)/30 = 18.421/30 = 0.614 /h; upper bound: ln(5e8)/30 = 20.030/30 = 0.668 /h; midpoint reported = 0.64 /h. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.64 = 1.08 h. CAVEAT: this is a colony-expansion population average on solid agar, NOT an exponential-phase mu (colony growth is growth-layer-limited and non-exponential), and includes lag; treat as a rough order-of-magnitude average only, hence low confidence. No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes are reported (physics/evolution study of clone-size distributions). LB composition not itemized in paper. A well-mixed control (~50 cells in 200 uL LB, incubated overnight to 10^8-10^9 cells) is reported but 'overnight' duration is unspecified so no rate is derivable. The color-switching budding yeast (S. cerevisiae yJK10, YPD, 30 C microfluidic) is a eukaryote and excluded.

growth_records.notes: Drug-free (0 µM chloramphenicol) exponential growth rate λ0 = β1 = 0.58 h-1, obtained by Kinbiont symbolic regression from OD600 time series fitted to λ=β1/(1+β2·d). growth_records.snippet: The cellular growth rates observed in our experiments range between 0.010 and 0.017 min-1. ... Cells were grown in M9 minimal medium supplemented with succinate (0.4%), casamino acids (0.01%), MgSO4 (3 mM), thiamine (30 mM), and uracil (10
growth_records.notes: Growth rate measured by single-cell microscopy (exponential fit to cell length over time), hence growth_method=calculated. Reported single-cell growth-rate range = 0.010-0.017 min-1. Converted to per-hour: 0.010 min-1 x 60 = 0.60 h-1; 0.017 min-1 x 60 = 1.02 h-1 -> mu = 0.60-1.02 h-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu: at 0.010 min-1, td = 0.6931/0.010 = 69.3 min = 1.155 h; at 0.017 min-1, td = 0.6931/0.017 = 40.8 min = 0.68 h -> td = 0.68-1.16 h. (Model illustration used non-measured example rates 0.005 and 0.02 min-1 for switching-probability calculation; not used here as they are model inputs, not measured growth.) Note: cost of full lac operon expression lowered growth rate ~6% (uninduced vs fully induced), not treated as a separate condition. No substrate uptake, product secretion, or yield values are reported in this paper (it measures fluorescence/switching, not metabolite fluxes). Carbon source = succinate 0.4% (w/v). Thiamine 30 mM and uracil 10 mM reported verbatim from Methods (Bacterial strains and culture conditions).

growth_records.snippet: L.B. EX_O2 (mmol/gDW/h) −11.5; U.B. EX_Ac (mmol/gDW/h) 3; δ (h–1) 0.03; V M Glc (mmol/gDW/h) 10; K M Glc (mM) 0.01; V M Ac (mmol/gDW/h) 10... generation time of 20 min
growth_records.notes: MODELING PAPER — values are dynamic-FBA model constraints, NOT measurements made in this study. Growth rate from assumed generation time 20 min: mu = ln2/(20/60 h) = 2.079 h-1; doubling = 0.333 h. O2 uptake (-11.5) and acetate secretion max (3 mmol/gDW/h) were calibrated to Varma & Palsson 1994 (ref 18) E. coli aerobic glucose batch data. Glucose/acetate Vmax=10 mmol/gDW/h with KM=0.01 mM are model MM parameters (from literature). Temperature/pH not stated. medium_composition_stated=false (M9 base not itemized). Flagged low confidence because provenance is secondary/model bounds.

growth_records.cite: Zhang et al. Nonlinear memory in cell-division dynamics across species. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (2025). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2417416122. [PMC12435308]
growth_records.snippet: Trajectories of cell sizes from 265 independent E. coli experiments are shown in light gray lines and a typical trajectory is highlighted. The doubling time is approximately 20 min.
growth_records.notes: This is a computational SDE-inference paper re-analyzing published mother-machine cell-size time series for E. coli, B. subtilis, D. discoideum, and S. pombe (data shared by Suckjoon Jun, Frank Bruggeman, Shigehiko Yumura, Yuichi Wakamoto). Only the E. coli generation/doubling time is stated quantitatively: 'The doubling time is approximately 20 min.' mu computed from doubling time: mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/(20/60 h) = 0.6931/0.3333 h = 2.08 /h. doubling_time_h = 20 min = 0.333 h. No medium composition, temperature, pH, or metabolic flux (uptake/secretion) values are reported in this paper (they reside in the original cited datasets). B. subtilis is also a prokaryote in this study but NO numeric doubling time or growth rate is given for it in the text (only qualitative 'sizer-adder' classification), so no B. subtilis record is emitted. S. pombe (yeast, td approx 2 h) and D. discoideum (amoeba) are eukaryotes and excluded. Confidence low: rate is approximate ('approximately 20 min') and no cultivation/medium metadata in-paper.

growth_records.notes: E. coli AB1157 (rpoS amber) single-cell time-lapse in a mother-machine microfluidic under continuous H2O2 (standard 100 uM; dose series 25/50/100 uM). Fully defined M9 minimal medium given verbatim (the listed KH2PO4/Na2HPO4/NaCl/NH4Cl values are the M9-salts stock concentrations before dilution). Growth is measured only as per-cell instantaneous elongation rate (log-difference in cell length between frames); NO numeric bulk mu or doubling time is reported in the text (values only in figures, e.g. Fig.2A elongation-rate traces). H2O2 stress transiently arrests growth. Therefore growth_rate_per_h and doubling_time_h left null. No substrate uptake or product secretion flux is quantified. Temperature 37 C from preculture/strain construction; imaging temperature not separately stated. pH not stated.

growth_records.snippet: exhibited a doubling time of 18 +/- 4 h in autotrophic conditions... formate-to-biomass conversion yield of 2.8 +/- 0.8 gCDW/mol formate... measured value for the formate uptake rate (19 +/- 2 mmol/gCDW/h)... measured growth rate (0.04 +/-
growth_records.notes: Growth rate reported directly as 0.04 +/- 0.01 h-1 (Fig S1) and doubling time 18 +/- 4 h (Fig 2C); consistent: mu = ln2/td = 0.693/18 = 0.0385 h-1. Formate uptake 19 +/- 2 mmol/gCDW/h measured directly. Biomass-formate yield Yxs = 2.8 +/- 0.8 gCDW/mol formate reported (Eq 1). Gross CO2 secretion derived: FDH oxidizes formate 1:1 to CO2, so gross CO2 = formate uptake = 19 mmol/gCDW/h; net is smaller due to Rubisco fixation (not given as single number). OD-to-biomass conversion used 0.4 +/- 0.1 gCDW/L per OD600. Base M9 salts (Na2HPO4/KH2PO4/NaCl/NH4Cl/MgSO4) not explicitly listed in paper; only trace elements + formate given.

growth_records.cite: Stricker et al. A fast, robust, and tunable synthetic gene oscillator. Nature (2008). DOI:10.1038/nature07389. [PMC6791529]
growth_records.snippet: The cell doubling time on the microfluidic device remained largely steady between experiments, ranging from 22.3-27.6 minutes at 37°C ... doubling time in minimal medium was significantly longer than in LB (80-90 minutes versus 22-24 minute
growth_records.notes: Doubling time in LB at 37°C reported directly as 22.3-27.6 min (single-cell microfluidic; text also states 22-24 min). mu = ln2/td. Using midpoint td = (22.3+27.6)/2 = 24.95 min = 0.4158 h => mu = 0.693/0.4158 = 1.667 /h. Range: td 22.3 min (0.3717 h) => mu 1.865 /h; td 27.6 min (0.460 h) => mu 1.507 /h. doubling_time_h reported as midpoint 0.416 h. growth_method=calculated (mu derived from reported doubling time). Medium named LB but composition not stated in paper, so composition [] and stated=false. No uptake/secretion fluxes reported (synthetic-biology oscillator study). Inducers arabinose 0.1-3% + IPTG 0-30 mM (e.g., 0.7% arabinose, 2 mM IPTG for 40-min period example).

growth_records.notes: E. coli S1030 host cells maintained in a chemostat for PACE. Dilution rate D = flow/volume = 400 mL/h / 250 mL = 1.6 /h. At chemostat steady state mu = D = 1.6 /h. Doubling time td = ln(2)/mu = 0.693/1.6 = 0.433 h (~26 min); feasible in this glucose+casamino-acids rich Davis medium at 37 C (below E. coli mu_max ~2 /h, no washout). Temperature 37 C taken from associated infection/culture steps. pH not stated for chemostat (theophylline-vehicle pH used only for lagoon equilibration in one experiment). Downstream lagoons operate at D=100/40=2.5 /h but host cells transit without net growth there (phage replicate), so not recorded as a bacterial growth condition. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates measured.

growth_records.snippet: All experiments were conducted using Escherichia coli MG1655 grown in M9 minimal media supplemented with 1 g/l of casaminoacids, 2 g/l of glucose, and 5% of glycerol. All cultures were grown in 96-well plates with a volume of 200 ul, incuba
growth_records.notes: E. coli MG1655 antibiotic-resistance experimental-evolution study, not a physiology/flux study. Medium supplements explicitly stated (casaminoacids 1 g/L, glucose 2 g/L, glycerol 5% v/v); the M9 base salt composition (Na2HPO4/KH2PO4/NaCl/NH4Cl/MgSO4/CaCl2) is NOT enumerated in the text. Growth conditions explicit: 30 C, 150 rpm orbital shaking, 96-well plates, aerobic, batch (1:5 or 1:50 serial dilution). Growth rates of drug-free evolved clones were reported (fitderiv/Swain 2016; mild-selection clones mean=10.59, s.e.=0.332, n=29; strong-selection clones mean=9.69, s.e.=0.199, n=89) but NO units are given in the article and the magnitude (~10) is not interpretable as a specific growth rate in h^-1 (E. coli mu on M9+glucose is ~0.6-1.0 /h); therefore growth_rate_per_h left null rather than fabricate a unit. No doubling time, no qS, no secretion/product fluxes, and no yields are reported. Also note separate handling steps at 30 C with 230 rpm and 14 h LB Broth-Lennox (Condalab) cultures for competition assays, but these carry no rate data either.

growth_records.cite: Hansen et al. Antibiotics can be used to contain drug-resistant bacteria by maintaining sufficiently large sensitive populations. PLoS Biology (2020). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.3000713. [PMC7266357]
growth_records.snippet: r_S | Intrinsic per capita growth rate of drug-sensitive strain | 0.0169 min^-1 ... Real-time per capita growth rate ... estimated from flow rates required to maintain constant cell density at each drug concentration.
growth_records.notes: Intrinsic (drug-free) per-capita growth rate of the sensitive ancestor REL607 reported in Table 1 as r_S = 0.0169 min^-1. Converted to per h: 0.0169 * 60 = 1.014 h^-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/1.014 = 0.684 h. Growth rate measured in bioreactor as media influx rate needed to hold constant density; time-averaged between 200-300 min post-drug at steady state (here drug=0). Medium = Davis Minimal Media (Sigma), full salt composition not enumerated in paper (commercial product) so medium_composition_stated=false; liquid overnight/working medium stated as Davis Minimal Media with 1,000 ug/mL glucose (paper prints '1,000 g/ml', a units typo for ug/mL; agar plates used 2,000 ug/mL). Bioreactor experiments run in a temperature-controlled warm room at 30 C. No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported.

growth_records.notes: Intrinsic (drug-free) per-capita growth rate of the doxycycline-resistant REL606-derived mutant reported in Table 1 as r_R = 0.0152 min^-1. Converted to per h: 0.0152 * 60 = 0.912 h^-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.912 = 0.760 h. Value is ~10% below the sensitive strain (r_S=0.0169 min^-1), the reported small fitness cost. Same bioreactor/medium/temperature (Davis Minimal Media + ~1000 ug/mL glucose, 30 C) as the sensitive record; full Davis salt composition not enumerated so medium_composition_stated=false. No uptake/secretion fluxes reported.

growth_records.cite: Okano et al. Regulation underlying hierarchical and simultaneous utilization of carbon substrates by flux sensors in Escherichia coli. Nat Microbiol (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41564-019-0610-7. [PMC6925339]
growth_records.snippet: the growth rate on glycerol only (≈ 0.7/h) ... the total carbon-uptake flux ... remained approximately constant ... at a level corresponding to that of growth on glycerol alone, (23 ± 1)ℂ (error represents 95% CI, n = 11) ... where ℂ =1 mM
growth_records.notes: E. coli NCM3722 growth on glycerol as sole carbon source. mu ≈ 0.7 h-1 (reported). Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.7 = 0.99 h (calculated). Uptake: paper's flux unit ℂ = 1 mM carbon-atoms per OD600 per hour; total C-uptake on glycerol alone = 23±1 ℂ. Biomass conversion (Methods): 1 OD600·mL = 0.44 mg DW → OD600 of 1 = 0.44 gDW/L. Specific C-uptake = 23 mmol C L-1 h-1 (per OD600) ÷ 0.44 gDW L-1 = 52.3 mmol C/gDW/h. Glycerol has 3 C → glycerol uptake = 52.3/3 = 17.4 mmol glycerol/gDW/h. Threshold flux jth ≈ 25 ℂ for glucose (strain NQ1243) and lactose (strain NQ917) marks the crossover between hierarchical and simultaneous utilization; glucose-only and lactose-only mu not numerically stated (only 'never below 0.7/h' and crossover growth rate 0.7-0.8/h), so not emitted as separate records to avoid fabrication. Carbon substrates supplied at 120 mM C-atoms for growth-rate measurements; N-C- base salt composition given by reference (Ref 37), not enumerated in text. pH not stated.

growth_records.snippet: knocking out GOGAT substantially slowed assimilation of 15N-ammonium into glutamine, with flux through glutamine reduced from 54 mM/min to 13 mM/min... consistent with 41 mM/min of glutamine feeding into GOGAT in wild type, and generating 8
growth_records.notes: Wild-type E. coli NCM 3722 grown on Gutnick minimal salts (cited Gutnick et al 1969, full salts composition NOT stated in text) + 10 mM NH4Cl + 0.4% glucose, 37C, filter culture on agarose. Doubling time 58 min reported (Fig 2A). growth_rate mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/(58/60 h) = 0.6931/0.9667 = 0.717 /h (calculated); doubling_time_h = 58/60 = 0.967 h. pH~7 stated later in text ('our experiments were conducted at pH~7'). No glucose uptake or by-product secretion rates measured, so uptake_rates/secretion_rates empty. mfa_fluxes are intracellular kinetic-flux-profiling fluxes in mM/min (glutamine/glutamate synthesis via GS/GOGAT), NOT gDW-normalized exchange fluxes; captured verbatim with arithmetic the authors give (54->13 mM/min on GOGAT KO; 54-13=41 mM/min into GOGAT; 41x2=82 mM/min glutamate).

growth_records.cite: Jie Yuan et al. Metabolomics-driven quantitative analysis of ammonia assimilation in E. coli. Molecular Systems Biology (2009). DOI:10.1038/msb.2009.60. [PMC2736657]
growth_records.snippet: growth of the culture did not stop, but continued at a reduced rate for ~1.5 additional doublings (doubling time ~220 min)... Consistent with the culture being nitrogen-limited, the GS protein content was elevated
growth_records.notes: Same WT NCM 3722, filter culture, but 2 mM NH4Cl initial (N-limited). During limitation doubling time ~220 min reported (Fig 2A, cyan). mu = ln2/(220/60 h) = 0.6931/3.6667 = 0.189 /h (calculated); doubling_time_h = 220/60 = 3.667 h. Earlier in this run cells grew at the unlimited rate (~58 min doubling) until OD650~0.4 before N ran out; the reduced-rate 220-min value characterizes the N-limited steady phase. No exchange uptake/secretion rates measured.

growth_records.cite: Jie Yuan et al. Metabolomics-driven quantitative analysis of ammonia assimilation in E. coli. Molecular Systems Biology (2009). DOI:10.1038/msb.2009.60. [PMC2736657]
growth_records.snippet: The growth rate was partially restored (doubling time ~117 min) by transferring the ammonium-limited filter culture to plates with 10 mM ammonium (Figure 2A, blue dots)
growth_records.notes: WT NCM 3722 filter culture, N-limited then upshifted to 10 mM NH4Cl. Post-upshift doubling time ~117 min (Fig 2A, blue). mu = ln2/(117/60 h) = 0.6931/1.95 = 0.355 /h (calculated); doubling_time_h = 117/60 = 1.95 h. Growth only partially restored vs the 58-min N-replete rate because cells previously experienced N-limitation (reduced max growth rate, tau0 = 90 min in model). No exchange rates measured.

Glucose 1.6% is % mass (g/g) per Methods; ~16 g/L approximate. pH adjusted to 6.8 with NaOH/HCl. The matched strain carrying the complete logic circuit reduced acetate accumulation during exponential growth (Fig 4C). No biomass or rate reported; qP not derivable.

growth_records.snippet: After cultivation for 41.7 h, the pcnB i - acrD i - fadR + strain achieved a remarkable FFAs production of 35.1 g L-1, with a productivity of 0.84 g L-1 h-1 and a yield of 0.20 g g-1 glycerol. ... Fermentation temperature was set at 30 C an
growth_records.notes: Metabolic-engineering (CRISPRi) paper, NOT a 13C-MFA study; no MFA fluxes. Only measured extracellular physiology rate is the FFA volumetric productivity. Titer=35.1 g/L FFA at 41.7 h in a 5 L bioreactor (1.8 L working vol), fed-batch, 30 C, pH 7 (controlled with 6 N NH4OH). Volumetric FFA productivity reported = 0.84 g/L/h; arithmetic check 35.1 g/L / 41.7 h = 0.842 g/L/h (matches). Product/substrate yield reported = 0.20 g FFA per g glycerol (glycerol is the carbon source; batch 15 g/L glycerol + fed 500 g/L glycerol feed). NO growth rate (mu), doubling time, biomass (gDW) time course, or specific substrate uptake rate (qS in mmol/gDW/h) is reported, so uptake_rate per gDW is NOT derivable and left is_experimental:false/null. Precursor titers reported only as endpoint amounts (mg/L, no rate): CF baseline 747 mg/L; pcnB_956 2916 mg/L; pcnB^i 2992 mg/L; pcnB^i-acrD^i-fadR^+ shake-flask 5105 mg/L (all in modified M9, glass-tube/shake-flask, 30 C, 250 rpm, 40 h post-IPTG, glycerol 30 g/L or glucose 30 g/L for Fig.4) - not emitted as separate records because no rate is derivable beyond a crude titer/time and no biomass data. Comparative titers cited from other organisms (Y. lipolytica 9.67 g/L, P. pastoris 23.4 g/L, S. cerevisiae 33.4 g/L, O. polymorpha 38.2 g/L, R. opacus 50.2 g/L) are literature citations, not measured here.

E. coli NCM3722-derived strain LO1 grown in MOPS glucose defined medium, aerobic batch, 37 C, pH 7.4. Reported maximal batch growth rate (lambda_s) spans 0.65-1.60 h-1 across different carbon/nitrogen substrates (per-substrate values in SI Table S7, not in XML). This record uses the fastest (glucose-type) condition: doubling time ~26 min reported for the Fig 4 single-cell tracking experiment. mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/(26/60 h) = 0.6931/0.4333 h = 1.600 h-1, matching the stated upper bound 1.60 h-1; doubling_time_h = 26/60 = 0.4333 h. No substrate uptake, product secretion, or 13C-MFA fluxes are reported (glucose consumption enters only qualitatively via lambda_s). Other lambda values (e.g. 0.65 h-1 lower bound) correspond to poorer substrates not individually specified in main text. Exact glucose concentration and full trace-metal (NH4)6Mo7O24 value truncated/not stated in extracted XML; medium base is the Neidhardt (1974) MOPS formulation cited in Methods.

growth_records.snippet: The chemostats were operated at a dilution rate of 0.15 h-1 corresponding to 25% of the maximum growth rate μMAX... arabinose in which 99% of all carbon atoms were the stable 13C isotope, and glucose in which 97% of all hydrogen atoms were
growth_records.notes: Steady-state chemostat: mu = D = 0.15 h-1 (experimental). Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.15 = 4.62 h (calculated). muMAX derived: 0.15 = 25% of muMAX -> muMAX = 0.15/0.25 = 0.60 h-1 (exact muMAX value in S2 File, not main text). This is a single-cell NanoSIMS assimilation study — NO bulk mmol/gDW/h uptake or secretion rates are reported; only relative single-cell 13C-arabinose / 2H-glucose incorporation. Feed sugar concentrations and chemostat temperature/pH not restated in main text (precultures 37°C, M9 + 3 mM Glc + 3 mM Ara + 5% LB).

up.units: n/a (arabinose uptake blocked at high sugar via catabolite repression; consumed sequentially)

yields.note: no yield reported; batch comparator condition

growth_records.snippet: A single colony was inoculated overnight at 37°C in minimal media containing 1x M9 salts, 1 mM MgSO4 and 0.1 mM CaCl2, supplemented with 3 mM D-glucose (Glc), 3 mM L-arabinose (Ara) and 5% (v/v) LB complex broth.
growth_records.notes: Batch condition (M9 + 3 mM Glc + 3 mM Ara + 5% LB, 37°C aerobic). No direct growth-rate value stated in main text for batch; growth_rate_per_h = muMAX = 0.60 h-1 (DERIVED: chemostat D 0.15 = 25% of muMAX). Doubling time td = ln2/0.60 = 1.16 h (calculated). Variation in growth rate >3x lower in batch than chemostat. No quantitative uptake/secretion flux; at high sugar glucose is used first and arabinose uptake is catabolite-repressed (sequential utilization). Low confidence because muMAX assigned to batch is inferred, not a batch-specific measurement.

No specific substrate uptake or product secretion measured for the base strain. Methanol evaporation ~2-3 mM/h at 37 C stated as a caveat, not an uptake rate.

growth_records.snippet: MEcoli_ref_2 grew exponentially to an OD600 of 100.2 (22.1 ± 0.5 g CDW l−1 (mean ± s.d.; CDW, cell dry weight)) at a doubling time of 4.9 h. At the achieved biomass concentration, oxygen transfer became limiting.
growth_records.notes: Fed-batch bioreactor, pH 7.0 (auto 4 M NH3), pO2 40%, 37 C. Measured: Td=4.9 h and final biomass 22.1±0.5 g CDW/L at OD600 100.2. mu=ln2/Td=0.6931/4.9=0.1414 /h (calculated). Methanol fed to maintain concentration, so no specific uptake rate available.

growth_records.snippet: Growth and induction of the ldh-expressing strain in minimal medium supplemented with 500 mM methanol resulted in a maximum titre of 284.0 ± 23.6 mg l−1 (3.2 ± 0.3 mM) for lactic acid with a carbon yield of 15.7 ± 1.0% (0.147 ± 0.009 g lact
growth_records.notes: Measured secretion of lactic acid from methanol: titre 284.0±23.6 mg/L (=3.2±0.3 mM) at 22 h; volumetric productivity 12.7±1.0 mg/L/h; carbon yield 15.7±1.0% (mass yield 0.147±0.009 g/g MeOH). 13C-methanol labelling confirmed 97% product labelling (product genuinely from methanol). Units left volumetric (mg/L/h) as reported; conversion to mmol/gDW/h not possible without biomass time-course. No growth rate reported for this plasmid-bearing condition.

growth_records.snippet: The strain delta-frmA delta-fbp (n=1) was evolved for 38 generations with a generation time of 25 h in the first 7 generations, 20 h for the next 6 generations and 15 h for the remaining generations.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat adaptive evolution; growth improved over time. Generation (doubling) times reported: 25 h (gen 1-7), 20 h (gen 8-13), 15 h (thereafter). mu = ln2/td: 0.693/25=0.028 h-1 (initial), 0.693/20=0.035 h-1, 0.693/15=0.046 h-1 (evolved). Reported representative = initial td 25 h / mu 0.028 h-1; evolved reaches mu ~0.046 h-1. No uptake/secretion rates measured. pH 7.0 (1M NH3 / 1M H2SO4 control).

growth_records.snippet: Comparison of growth curves on MOPS minimal medium with 0.2% N-acetylglucosamine showed that E. coli HS delta-nagE had a doubling time of 2.6 h, compared to 1 h doubling time for the wild type.

growth_records.notes: Fully-defined prokaryote batch growth condition; medium composition explicitly stated. E. coli used only as a factory to make 13C isotopologue standards on acetate as sole C source; growth monitored by OD600 (biomass conversion 0.37 gDW/OD) but NO growth rate, uptake or secretion rate is reported, so all rate fields null/empty. The 13C-MFA fluxes elsewhere in this paper are for Brassica napus leaves (a plant/eukaryote, U-13C-pyruvate labeling of leaf discs) and are therefore EXCLUDED from prokaryote records. Recorded here for its GEM-relevant defined acetate minimal-medium condition.

growth_records.snippet: Model parameters estimated using E. coli cell lineage data at three different temperatures ... g [exponential growth rate of cell size] = 0.0123 +/- 0.0001 (25C). The time course data of the cell length were recorded every minute for 279 ce
growth_records.notes: Growth rate g (Table 3, Model I, 25C) = 0.0123 +/- 0.0001 per minute, obtained by optimal fit of the theoretical to the EXPERIMENTAL doubling-time distribution of E. coli lineage data (paper states g cannot be inferred from size distribution and is determined from doubling-time fit). Units are per minute (cell length recorded every minute; T=(1/g)ln(Vd/Vb) yields cell-cycle durations in minutes). For exponentially growing single cells the cell-size growth rate equals the specific growth rate mu. mu = 0.0123/min x 60 = 0.738 /h. Doubling time td = ln2/g = 0.6931/0.0123 = 56.35 min = 0.939 h. No substrate uptake, product secretion, or MFA fluxes reported (cell-size/homeostasis theory paper). Underlying single-cell data cited from Tanouchi et al. 2015 (Cell Reports) and Tanouchi et al. 2017; medium composition and strain not stated in this paper.
