Doubling time td = ln2/g = 0.6931/0.0249 = 27.84 min = 0.464 h, consistent with classic ~28 min E. coli doubling at 37C. Cell-size growth rate = specific growth rate for exponential single-cell growth. No substrate uptake, product secretion, or MFA fluxes reported. Strain and medium composition not stated; underlying single-cell data cited from Tanouchi et al. 2015 (Cell Reports).

Two parallel measurements of specific growth rate: MAGI device mu=2.34 +/- 0.2 h-1 (generation time 17.8 +/- 1.8 min = 0.297 h); OD600 mu=2.11 +/- 0.16 h-1 (generation time 19.8 +/- 1.4 min = 0.330 h); not significantly different (p=0.08). Primary growth_rate_per_h reports the MAGI value 2.34; the OD600 value 2.11 (td 0.330 h) is an alternative measurement of the same condition. Doubling_time_h=0.297 = 17.8 min/60 (reported); consistent with ln2/2.34=0.296 h. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates measured (growth-instrument methods paper). growth_records.snippet: The dilution rate (setting the generation time) was set to four hours during the first month and two hours thereafter. ... the value for the growth rate at equilibrium is the dilution rate like in a classical chemostat.
growth_records.notes: Thymine-limited glucose chemostat of E. coli β7328. Generation (doubling) time set to 4 h during the first month. In a chemostat at steady state µ=D and D=ln2/td, so µ=D=ln2/4 h=0.6931/4=0.1733 h⁻¹; doubling_time_h=4 (as stated). No substrate uptake or product secretion rates were measured in this paper (device/experimental-evolution study). pH not stated. Glucose stated as 10 mM in Results (chemostat inoculum in MS medium); Methods notes routine MS medium with 2 g glucose/L for plates/batch. Thymine 1 µM (growth-limiting); 5 µM stated as minimum for optimal proliferation, 10 µM used as excess for batch max-growth-rate measurements (numeric µ not given in text, only Fig 4). MS base salts composition given only by reference [24], not in text.

Growth rate CALCULATED: mu = qs,glu * Yx/s = 0.25 g/g/h * 0.37 g/g = 0.0925 /h; doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.0925 = 7.49 h (applies to the qs,glu=0.25 biomass fed-batch phase common to all four strains). qs,glu setpoint controlled via feed. Unit conversions (mmol/gDW/h): glucose 0.25 g/g/h /180.16*1000 = 1.39; qs,glu,crit 0.88 -> 4.88; lactose (MW 342.3) qs,lac,max* 0.23 -> 0.672; qs,lac,noglu 0.023 -> 0.067. Batch phase at 35 C (biomass 8-9 g DCW/L), induction phase at 30 C (temperature_C=30 reported here). Glucose accumulation observed above qs,glu ~0.32 g/g/h. qs,lac,max* and qs,glu,crit are mechanistic-model fits to experimental lactose-uptake measurements.

growth_records.snippet: TA2463 produced 37.7 +/- 1.6 mM 3-HP and the OD600 and glycerol consumption at 48 h were 4.58 +/- 0.18, 163.9 +/- 1.8 mM, respectively. The 3-HP yield from glycerol was 26.5 +/- 1.1 % (mol/mol). A significant amount of 1,3-propanediol (12.8
growth_records.notes: Reference synthetic-pathway strain (no MTS repression), IPTG 0.1 mM added at 0 h. Measured at 48 h: 3-HP 37.7+/-1.6 mM, 1,3-PDO 12.8+/-1.8 mM, glycerol consumed 163.9+/-1.8 mM, OD600 4.58+/-0.18, acetate max 2.18 mM (24 h, then reassimilated), lactate not detected. Yield reported 26.5+/-1.1 % mol 3-HP/mol glycerol; my cross-check 37.7/163.9 = 0.230 (23.0%) differs from stated 26.5% (authors likely referenced glycerol consumed at a different sampling point) - stated value retained. Volumetric rates derived by dividing endpoint titer/consumption by 48 h (batch runtime). qS in mmol/gDW/h NOT computable: no OD600-to-gDW conversion factor given. Not a 13C-MFA paper; authors state 13C-MFA is future work; LC-MS gave only relative (fold-change) intracellular metabolite levels, not fluxes.

growth_records.snippet: We grew E. coli MG1655 and V. natriegens ATCC14048 in their respective anoxic minimal media with 25 mM glucose as the sole carbon source... E. coli consumed 1.2 times more glucose... final E. coli pH was more acidic at 4.8 +/- 0.0 compared
growth_records.notes: Anoxic mixed-acid fermentation of E. coli MG1655 vs V. natriegens (paired experiment, same cultures for Fig 2/3/4). Medium MDC = M9-derived (ref 63) with 25 mM glucose, 10 mM NH4Cl, 1 mM MgSO4, 0.1 mM CaCl2; 37C, 150 rpm. Absolute growth rate (h^-1), absolute glucose-consumed (mM), and absolute product concentrations/yields are reported ONLY in figures (Fig 2C exponential growth rate; Fig 4A glucose consumed; Fig 3A yields) and are NOT stated numerically in text, so left null. Explicit in-text numbers captured: succinic acid ~1.3 mM, transient pyruvate ~0.8 mM, alpha-ketoglutarate ~0.8 mM (E. coli). Fold comparisons vs V. natriegens: glucose consumed 1.2x higher, acetic acid yield 1.20x higher, lactic acid 1.06x lower, formic acid & ethanol no significant difference. Final pH 4.8 +/- 0.0 at 24 h. Carbon balance: 5 +/- 1% unaccounted. E. coli grew 1.3x SLOWER than V. natriegens (V. natriegens grew 1.3x faster despite lower temperature). No mmol/gDW/h rate derivable from text alone (biomass-carbon conversion and time-resolved slopes only in figures).

growth_records.snippet: Slow-growing cells at steady state in a carbon-limited continuous culture (D = mu = 0.072 h-1, biomass concentration approx. 4 g L-1)... The pH was kept at pH 6.6 and the dissolved oxygen concentration (pO2) was always above 40% air saturat
growth_records.notes: Steady-state glucose-limited chemostat of E. coli LJ110 at D=mu=0.072/h, 28 C, pH 6.6, aerobic (pO2>40%), glucose feed 8 g/L, biomass X~4 g/L. Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.072=9.63 h. Glucose uptake derived from chemostat carbon balance assuming residual glucose ~0 (carbon-limited): qGlc = D*(Sf-S)/X = 0.072*(8-0)/4 = 0.144 g/gDW/h; /180.16 g/mol *1000 = 0.80 mmol/gDW/h. Biomass yield Yxs ~4 gDW/L / 8 g/L ~0.5 gDW/g (derived, assuming full glucose consumption). RQ at steady state ~1.1 mol/mol (fully respiratory, no overflow) so acetate secretion negligible at steady state -> secretion_rates empty. The glucose-pulse transient that follows is a perturbation, not a distinct steady growth condition, so not emitted as a separate record. Medium described only as 'defined medium essentially as described previously [ref 12]'; full composition not given in this paper (medium_composition_stated=false).

growth_records.cite: Chuang et al. Dissecting the disconnect between circuit activation and dominant adaptive evolution in cytoplasmic phage-assisted continuous evolution (PACE) of an EGFR nanobody. Frontiers in Bioengineering and Biotechnology (2026). DOI:10.3389/fbioe.2026.1829373. [PMC13260496]

growth_records.notes: E. coli is the host chassis for a phage-assisted continuous evolution (PACE) study, NOT the biological subject; the paper reports no substrate uptake, product secretion, or MFA fluxes. Only one growth parameter is derivable: the chemostat dilution rate D = 1 V/h (=1 vessel volume per hour = 1 /h). At chemostat steady state mu = D, so growth_rate_per_h = 1.0 /h (calculated, not measured). Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/1.0 = 0.693 h. Medium 2xYT is undefined-composition rich broth (composition not given numerically), so medium_composition_stated=false. Temperature 37 C used throughout the study (transformations, plaque assays, host cell growth). pH not stated for the chemostat. Aerobic assumed (aerobic E. coli culture) though aeration not quantified. No Yxs, no experimental rates available.

growth_records.snippet: The β-alanine production from FAs was 2.52 ± 0.06 g/l (strain BA72)... The yield of β-alanine from FA-using strain BA72 was approximately 0.71 ± 0.01 g/g... productivity of 1.42 g/l/h, higher than that of BA72 (1.03 g/l/h).
growth_records.notes: Engineered E. coli producing beta-alanine from fatty acids via glyoxylate-TCA cycle. BA72 = FA09 chassis + TcpanD + aspA up + iclR KO. Product (beta-alanine) secretion: fed-batch volumetric productivity 1.03 g/L/h; 3 h whole-cell bioconversion on palmitic acid gave 2.52 g/L (=>0.84 g/L/h) at 0.71 g/g yield. No specific growth rate reported (two-stage: growth on glucose then non-growing FA bioconversion). Substrate (palmitic acid) consumption measured by GC (Fig 2B) but no numeric qS in text. Yields are g product/g substrate (experimental).

growth_records.snippet: the strain BA92, with only fumAC knocked-out, could even produce β-alanine with 1.10 ± 0.05 g/g yield in the flask cultivation, but only produced β-alanine with a yield of 0.4 g/g in the fed-batch mode. The titer of β-alanine was only about
growth_records.notes: BA92 = fumAC knockout (TCA blocked). High flask yield (1.10 g/g) but poor fed-batch (0.4 g/g, 25.88 g/L in 43 h => 0.60 g/L/h derived) with acetate overflow (13.62 g/L) and impaired growth. Off-site two-stage bioconversion restored 1.1 g/g in 20 h. Illustrates growth/production trade-off of TCA block. No growth rate reported.

growth_records.strain: K-12 MG1655, pre-evolved reference, delta-pgi evolved endpoint ePgi_1 (evolved knockout, ALE)
growth_records.cite: McCloskey, Douglas et al. Evolution of gene knockout strains of E. coli reveal regulatory architectures governed by metabolism. Nature Communications (2018). DOI:10.1038/s41467-018-06219-9. [PMC6143558]
growth_records.notes: Fitness reported by the authors is the specific growth rate in h-1 (ALE fitness = growth rate on glucose M9). Value 0.68 +/- 0.006 h-1 for ePgi endpoint 1. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.68 = 1.019 h. Culture: M9 minimal medium with 4 g/L glucose, 37 C, well-mixed flasks (aerobic), serial-passage ALE (batch). Full M9 salt/trace-element concentrations not enumerated in article text (cited to standard M9 recipe). Substrate uptake and product secretion rates WERE measured (culture density at 600 nm; filtrate analyzed by refractive index/HPLC) and, together with 13C-MID data, used to constrain a genome-scale 13C-MFA model to compute absolute intracellular fluxes; however the numeric uptake/secretion/MFA flux values reside in Supplementary Data files, not in the article XML, so uptake_rates/secretion_rates/mfa_fluxes left empty.

growth_records.snippet: Glucose-limited chemostat cultures were grown using 1 l of synthetic MOPS medium containing 0.4% glucose and 19 mM NH4Cl. Cultures were grown aerobically at 37°C in a 2-l jar fermentor... The flow rate of air was maintained at 1 l/min, and
growth_records.notes: Growth rate not stated explicitly; at chemostat steady state mu = D, so mu = 0.6 /h. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.6 = 1.155 h. Medium: 0.4% (w/v) glucose = 4 g/L (=~22.2 mM, MW 180.16); full MOPS salt composition is only given by citation (Neidhardt et al., ref 48) so only the two explicitly stated components (glucose, 19 mM NH4Cl) are listed. This is a ribosome-profiling/mRNA-seq study; NO substrate uptake or product secretion rates and NO MFA fluxes are reported in this paper. A cited earlier study (ref 3) quantified 55 central-carbon enzymes/mRNAs at D=0.7 and D=0.5 /h but those rates belong to that other paper, not this one.

growth_records.strain: JA122 (mutator + tRNA suppressor; derivative background of K-12 MG1655)
growth_records.cite: Schwartz, Katja et al. Adaptive genetics reveals constraints on protein structure/function by evolving E. coli under constant nutrient limitation. BMC Biology (2025). DOI:10.1186/s12915-025-02331-7. [PMC12366229]
growth_records.snippet: Briefly, using Davis Minimal Medium, E. coli JA122 was evolved in triplicate in aerobic, glucose-limited (0.0125% w/v) chemostats at a fixed dilution rate of 0.2 h-1 and at a constant temperature of 30 degC.
growth_records.notes: Genomics/experimental-evolution paper; the only experimental prokaryote growth condition is the chemostat operating setpoint. At chemostat steady state growth_rate mu = dilution_rate D = 0.2 /h (reported D=0.2 h^-1). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.2 = 3.47 h (calculated). Glucose 0.0125% w/v = 0.125 g/L (0.0125 g/100 mL); reported as concentration, not as a specific uptake rate. Full Davis Minimal Medium salt composition not itemized in this paper (referenced to prior work ref 25), so medium_composition_stated=false. No measured specific substrate-uptake, product-secretion, MFA fluxes, or biomass yields are reported in this article (it is a whole-genome whole-population sequencing / adaptive-genetics study). NOTE: other dilution rates appearing in the text (mu=0.12 h^-1 and mu=0.6 h^-1) are CITED from another study (ref 126) comparing flagellar expression at slow vs fast dilution, NOT experimental conditions of this paper, so they are excluded.

growth_records.snippet: the double knockout strain TH 3-3 achieved the highest L-theanine titer, reaching 5.44 +/- 0.07 g/L, which corresponds to a yield of 0.19 g/g xylose and represents a 16.2-fold increase over that of strain TH 2.
growth_records.notes: L-theanine degradation eliminated (delta ansB delta ggt). Titer 5.44 +/- 0.07 g/L, yield 0.19 g/g xylose. Xylose consumed DERIVED = titer/yield = 5.44/0.19 = 28.6 g/L (consistent with ~30 g/L initial). Endpoint measurements; no time-resolved mu.

growth_records.snippet: strain TH 4-4, which overexpresses GDH-4, achieved the highest L-theanine titer of 9.94 +/- 0.18 g/L, a 68.8% increase relative to the control strain TH 3-3, with a yield of 0.33 g/g.
growth_records.notes: Final engineered strain under standard aerobic shake-flask conditions. Titer 9.94 +/- 0.18 g/L, yield 0.33 g/g xylose. Xylose consumed DERIVED = 9.94/0.33 = 30.1 g/L. Endpoint measurements. This is the same strain later run two-stage and in fed-batch (separate records).

up.method: derived from yield: 14.31 g/L theanine / 0.48 g/g

growth_records.cite: Han, Haolin et al. Systematic Metabolic Engineering and Model-Guided Optimization for High-Level Production of L-Theanine from Xylose in Escherichia coli. Advanced Science (Adv Sci, Weinh) (2026). DOI:10.1002/advs.202521440. [PMC13042605]
growth_records.snippet: This two-stage strategy effectively enhanced L-theanine production, achieving a titer of 14.31 +/- 0.21 g/L with a yield of 0.48 g/g xylose, which is 1.44 times higher than that under aerobic cultivation conditions.
growth_records.notes: Two-stage microaerobic-aerobic DO strategy (plus sucAB attenuation). Titer 14.31 +/- 0.21 g/L, yield 0.48 g/g xylose (1.44x over single-stage aerobic). Xylose consumed DERIVED = 14.31/0.48 = 29.8 g/L. Oxygen classified microaerophilic due to microaerobic growth phase; production phase aerobic. Endpoint measurements.

up.method: derived from yield: 95.42 g/L theanine / 0.55 g/g = 173.5 g/L; volumetric avg = 173.5/72 = 2.41 g/L/h

growth_records.cite: Han, Haolin et al. Systematic Metabolic Engineering and Model-Guided Optimization for High-Level Production of L-Theanine from Xylose in Escherichia coli. Advanced Science (Adv Sci, Weinh) (2026). DOI:10.1002/advs.202521440. [PMC13042605]
growth_records.snippet: L-theanine was accumulated rapidly after 4 h, reaching a peak concentration of 95.42 +/- 2.65 g/L at 72 h. The corresponding yield and productivity were 0.55 g/g xylose and 1.33 g/L/h, respectively. ... cell growth entered the stationary ph
growth_records.notes: Fed-batch 5 L bioreactor, strain TH4-4. Peak L-theanine 95.42 +/- 2.65 g/L at 72 h; yield 0.55 g/g xylose; productivity 1.33 g/L/h (reported; consistent with 95.42/72=1.325). Xylose consumed DERIVED = titer/yield = 95.42/0.55 = 173.5 g/L; avg volumetric xylose uptake = 173.5/72 = 2.41 g/L/h. Note initial medium also contains 10 g/L glucose + 20 g/L xylose; yield reported per g xylose. Biomass reached OD600 ~50 (stationary within 20 h) but no gDW conversion or time-resolved mu given, so specific rates in mmol/gDW/h not computable. pH controlled by 25% ammonium hydroxide (value not stated).

growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state: mu=D=0.09 h-1; doubling_time=ln2/0.09=7.702 h. No steady-state residual glucose, biomass, or product rates were tabulated, so no qs/qp derivable. Used for transcriptomics (temperature upshift 37->45 C).

growth_records.cite: Delvenne, Mathéo et al. Overriding Bioprocess Perturbations With a Cell-Machine Interface for Reliable Microbial Stress-Response Control. Microbial Biotechnology (2026). DOI:10.1111/1751-7915.70329. [PMC13075544]
growth_records.snippet: Chemostat conditions were applied at D = 0.3 h-1 for E. coli... with each condition maintained for at least five residence times. ...working volume of 1 L, an agitation speed of 1200 rpm for E. coli... and an aeration rate of 1 VVM. Tempera
growth_records.notes: E. coli glucose-limited chemostat. At steady state specific growth rate equals dilution rate: mu = D = 0.3 h-1 (calculated). Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.3 = 2.31 h. Aerobic (1 VVM), 37C, pH 7, 1200 rpm. Under Segregostat feedback control (1 g glucose/pulse triggered when >50% of population exceeded fluorescence threshold), the effective dilution rate transiently rose to ~0.33 h-1. No experimental substrate uptake or product secretion rates are reported in the text (study focuses on single-cell stress-response entropy via online flow cytometry; a cybernetic MONKS model computes fluxes but numeric qS/qP not given). Co-cultured organism in same paper, S. cerevisiae CEN.PK 113-7D (D=0.1 h-1), is a eukaryote and excluded.

growth_records.cite: Liebal, Ulf W. et al. CO2 to succinic acid – Estimating the potential of biocatalytic routes. Metabolic Engineering Communications (2018). DOI:10.1016/j.mec.2018.e00075. [PMC6127376]
growth_records.snippet: The experimental acetate uptake and succinate production rate were inferred from the slope of the time profiles of metabolite concentrations of an engineered E. coli mutant presented in Fig. 4 of Li et al. (2016) to 1.6 mmol/gCDW/h (acetate
growth_records.notes: CITED/SECONDARY rate: values are not measured in this (computational FBA) paper; they were inferred by the authors from Fig. 4 time-profiles of an engineered succinate-producing E. coli mutant reported in Li et al. (2016) [ref bib38, cited as 'Li et al., 2016']. Non-growing cells (mu=0), aerobic, acetate as sole C-source. Product yield computed from the two cited rates: Ysucc/acetate = 0.64 mmol/gCDW/h succinate / 1.6 mmol/gCDW/h acetate = 0.40 mol succinate per mol acetate. These rates were used to derive a non-growth NGAM of 1.36 mmol/gCDW/h (Table 2) via FBA on iJO1366. Temperature, pH, and medium composition are not given in this paper (see original Li et al. 2016). No genuinely new wet-lab prokaryote measurement exists in this modeling paper; all other tabulated rates (Synechocystis, C. ljungdahlii, P. pastoris, S. cerevisiae) are either FBA-simulated or borrowed constraints (e.g., C. ljungdahlii substrate/growth from Straub et al. 2014 A. woodii phenotype), hence not emitted as measured records.

growth_records.cite: van Heerden CD et al. Continuous and batch cultures of Escherichia coli KJ134 for succinic acid fermentation: metabolic flux distributions and production characteristics. Microbial Cell Factories (2013). DOI:10.1186/1475-2859-12-80. [PMC3848690]
growth_records.method: reported (max growth rate 0.22 h-1, abstract); td=ln2/0.22 calculated
growth_records.snippet: A maximum growth rate of 0.22 h-1 was obtained... final SA yield of 0.94 g g-1... maximum maintenance production rate of SA under batch conditions was found to be 0.6 g g-1 h-1. Growth terminated ~18 g/L SA, ~3.5 g/L AcA.
growth_records.notes: Batch, 90 g/L glucose (Table 3). Reported mu_max=0.22 h-1 (abstract); td=ln2/0.22=3.15 h. Growth terminates at ~18 g/L SA / ~3.5 g/L AcA. Non-growth maintenance SA production 0.6 g SA/gDW/h reported -> 0.6/118.09*1000=5.08 mmol/gDW/h (twice the chemostat maintenance 0.3 g/gDW/h = 2.54 mmol/gDW/h, from Fig 6 y-intercept). Final (214 h): glucose residual 9.8 g/L (from 90), SA 61.0 g/L, AcA 8.7 g/L, YSP up to 0.94 g/g, SA:AcA incremental up to 5. Specific qGlc not cleanly derivable (time-varying DCW).

up.method: derived qS=(Sin)*D/X assuming complete consumption of 21.9 mM feed glucose (carbon-limited)

sec.method: reported specific acetate production rate for the anaerobic steady state
derived qP=C*D/X from measured 35.1 mM (NMR) at anaerobic steady state

growth_records.cite: Trotter EW et al. Reprogramming of Escherichia coli K-12 Metabolism during the Initial Phase of Transition from an Anaerobic to a Micro-Aerobic Environment. PLoS ONE (2011). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0025501. [PMC3181329]
growth_records.snippet: the specific rate of acetate production for the anaerobic steady-state was ~8.2 mmoles h-1 g-1 cdw ... Time 0: Acetate 18.7 mM, Formate 35.1 mM, Succinate 5.3 mM, Pyruvate <0.10 mM, Lactate 0.12 mM, Ethanol 9.7 mM, Biomass 0.46 mg cdw ml-1
growth_records.notes: Anaerobic glucose-limited chemostat steady state, D=0.2 h-1 so mu=D=0.2 h-1; td=ln2/0.2=3.47 h. Biomass X=0.46 mg cdw/ml=0.46 g/L (Table 3, 44.3% C, carbon balance 96%). Steady-state secretion rate qP=C*D/X. Acetate: 18.7*0.2/0.46=8.13 (paper reports ~8.2, confirms). Formate: 35.1*0.2/0.46=15.26. Ethanol: 9.7*0.2/0.46=4.22. Succinate: 5.3*0.2/0.46=2.30. Lactate: 0.12*0.2/0.46=0.052. Glucose uptake qS=21.9*0.2/0.46=9.52 assuming complete consumption of feed (carbon-limited). Mixed-acid fermentation product profile. Only acetate rate explicitly stated in paper; others derived from measured NMR concentrations at steady state.

sec.method: reported specific acetate production rate for the 60-120 min micro-aerobic transition period
transient excretion; measured extracellular pyruvate peaked at 0.99 mM at 60 min (0.90 at 30, 0.41 at 10, <0.10 at 0), then net consumption by 120 min (0.23 mM)

growth_records.cite: Trotter EW et al. Reprogramming of Escherichia coli K-12 Metabolism during the Initial Phase of Transition from an Anaerobic to a Micro-Aerobic Environment. PLoS ONE (2011). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0025501. [PMC3181329]
growth_records.snippet: ~6.2 mmoles h-1 g-1 cdw for the 60-120 min period of the transition i.e. after transition acetate production was sub-maximal but non-zero, indicative of micro-aerobic growth ... transient excretion of pyruvate
growth_records.notes: Same chemostat (D=0.2 h-1, mu=0.2), but this is a dynamic (non-steady-state) transition from anaerobic to micro-aerobic (10 uM O2), so rates are transient. Acetate ~6.2 mmol/gDW/h explicitly reported for 60-120 min window (vs 8.2 anaerobic). Pyruvate transiently excreted (Table 3, mM): 0(t0),0.41(10),0.90(30),0.99(60),0.23(120) - peaks at 60 min then net consumed. Succinate/formate/ethanol concentrations decline over transition (Table 3). Biomass rises 0.46->0.65 mg cdw/ml over 120 min. Reported qAcetateMax anaerobic=0% aerobiosis reference.

growth_records.cite: Pal A et al. Global pleiotropic effects in adaptively evolved Escherichia coli lacking CRP reveal molecular mechanisms that define the growth physiology. Open Biology (2022). DOI:10.1098/rsob.210206. [PMC8846999]
growth_records.snippet: We carried out RNA-sequencing of the WT and the Dcrp cultivated in glucose-limited chemostat conditions at a fixed dilution rate of 0.21 h-1.
growth_records.notes: WT chemostat steady state: mu=D=0.21 h-1; td=ln2/0.21=3.30 h. Absolute GUR/APR/OUR/biomass-yield were measured but reported only in Figure 7 (image) and supplementary file S1 as strain-relative values, not as numeric absolutes in the article text, so uptake/secretion rates not extractable here. Batch (glucose-excess) aerobic cultures also run in M9+2 g/L glucose+40 mM MOPS, 37C, pH7.2, air 700 ml/min.

up.compound: glucose (measured with fluorescent analogue 2-NBDG, 30 uM in M9)
up.units: arbitrary fluorescence units (a.u.), single-cell whole-cell intensity
up.method: Single-cell time-lapse fluorescence of 2-NBDG uptake over 15 min (n=40 old/new pole daughter pairs); new pole daughters accumulate significantly more glucose than old pole daughters (paired t-test). Not convertible to mmol/gDW/h.

growth_records.cite: Lapinska U et al. Bacterial ageing in the absence of external stressors. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences (2019). DOI:10.1098/rstb.2018.0442. [PMC6792439]
growth_records.snippet: the parental cell divides asymmetrically, with the old pole daughter showing a longer doubling time and slower glucose accumulation than the new pole daughter ... doubling time ... progressively increases with successive rounds of cell divi
growth_records.notes: Single-cell ageing study of wild-type E. coli BW25113 in a mother machine under continuous LB perfusion at 37C. Growth physiology = per-cell doubling times measured over generations, but NO absolute doubling-time or growth-rate value is given in the text (data only in Fig 2 as generation-dependent curves): old pole daughters divide slower than new pole daughters (LMM chi2_1=5.192, p=0.023) and doubling time rises then asymptotes over 24 generations. Glucose uptake measured only as relative single-cell 2-NBDG fluorescence (a.u.), not as a rate; new pole > old pole. No numeric mu/qS/qP extractable, so all rate fields left null. LB composition: 10 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, 0.5 g/L NaCl. Additional strains (BW25113 tnaC-GFP, BL21 expressing Abeta1-42) were used only as positive controls for aggregate formation and reported no growth rates.

growth_records.medium: SMM (Standard Mineral Medium, ref [18]) + 10 mM glycerol

up.method: calculated average specific rate: delta-glycerol / (avg biomass x time)

growth_records.cite: Martin Demko et al. Computational Modelling of Metabolic Burden and Substrate Toxicity in Escherichia coli Carrying a Synthetic Metabolic Pathway. Microorganisms (2019). DOI:10.3390/microorganisms7110553. [PMC6921056]
growth_records.snippet: the particular results for CDW starting at 0.0429 and ending at 0.468 g/L after 10 h of growth; and (right) the result for the substrate utilisation only starting at 10.12 and ending at 0.07 mM after 10 h ... The experimental data are pictu
growth_records.notes: E. coli deg31 growth on SMM mineral medium with 10 mM glycerol as sole carbon source; glycerol is the harmless final product of the engineered TCP-degradation pathway and here serves as the growth substrate. Full SMM composition not given in-text (cited ref [18]); only 10 mM glycerol carbon source stated -> medium_composition_stated=false. pH not reported. MEASURED endpoints from Figure 6 model-fit-to-experimental-data (points are experimental): CDW 0.0429->0.468 g/L over 10 h; glycerol 10.12->0.07 mM over 10 h. Measured doubling-time range 2.58-9.29 h (median of >=3 experiments spanning IPTG induction 0-1 mM; metabolic-burden slows growth at higher IPTG). DERIVED: (1) growth_rate 0.239/h = ln(0.468/0.0429)/10h = ln(10.909)/10 = 2.3897/10 (apparent exponential mu from CDW endpoints); reported doubling-time range gives mu = ln2/td = 0.693/9.29 = 0.075/h (slow) to 0.693/2.58 = 0.269/h (fast); listed doubling_time_h=2.9 corresponds to the mu=0.239 endpoint estimate (0.693/0.239). (2) Glycerol volumetric uptake = (10.12-0.07 mM)/10 h = 1.005 mmol/L/h. (3) Specific glycerol uptake qS = 1.005 / avg_biomass, avg_biomass=(0.0429+0.468)/2=0.2555 g/L -> qS = 3.93 mmol/gDW/h (average over run). (4) Yxs = biomass produced / glycerol consumed = (0.468-0.0429 g/L)/(10.05e-3 mol/L x 92.09 g/mol) = 0.4251/0.9255 = 0.459 gDW/g glycerol (glycerol MW=92.09). Paper is primarily a modeling study but these growth/substrate values are its own real experimental data used for model calibration; no product secretion measured.

growth_records.cite: Perry EB et al. The Molecular and Genetic Basis of Repeatable Coevolution between Escherichia coli and Bacteriophage T3 in a Laboratory Microcosm. PLoS ONE (2015). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0130639. [PMC4482675]
growth_records.snippet: Chemostats were run ... with a resource feed of Davis Minimal media supplemented with glucose at a concentration of 1mg/mL. The volume of each chemostat was kept constant at 30mL, the dilution rate was set to 0.2 turnovers per hour, and the
growth_records.notes: Coevolution/genomics study of E. coli B (REL607) vs phage T3; NO measured substrate-uptake or product-secretion rates and no MFA reported. Only a defined chemostat growth condition. mu set equal to dilution rate at steady state: mu = D = 0.2 h^-1. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.2 = 3.47 h (calculated). Glucose feed 1 mg/mL = 1 g/L (~5.55 mM); Davis Minimal salt composition not stated in text (referenced). Reported cell densities were counts (~2x10^9 cells/mL when resource-limited, <10^7 when phage-limited), not gDW, so a mmol/gDW/h glucose uptake could not be derived. Phage T3 is a virus (not a prokaryote), so no separate record.

growth_records.snippet: E. coli, which is known to be a d-lactate producer, started to produce d-lactate after 10 h of mono-culture in M9; the same pattern was observed in the co-culture in this medium.
growth_records.notes: E. coli K-12 BW25113 mono-culture in M9 + 2% glucose (static, 37 C). E. coli is the only tested strain that grows in M9 mono-culture (Fig. S2), but no numeric µ is reported for E. coli. D-lactate secretion onset at ~10 h experimentally observed (Fig. S3); glucose is the carbon source. No numeric rates -> rate=null. Retained for the experimentally observed D-lactate excretion (overflow) product.

growth_records.cite: Park, Junyoung O. et al. Metabolite concentrations, fluxes, and free energies imply efficient enzyme usage. Nature Chemical Biology (2016). DOI:10.1038/nchembio.2077. [PMC4912430]
growth_records.snippet: Net flux through the oxidative pentose phosphate pathway was 20% of glucose uptake in E. coli... Flux of glucose-derived two-carbon units into the TCA cycle consumed 24% of pyruvate made in E. coli. E. coli K-12 strain NCM3722 was grown at
growth_records.notes: 13C-MFA/thermodynamics paper covering E. coli (prokaryote), yeast and mammalian iBMK; only E. coli recorded here. Absolute nutrient uptake and waste excretion rates were experimentally measured by 1H-NMR of media (rate of disappearance/appearance corrected for biomass and growth rate; conversion factors given: 1.0 OD600 = 0.47 gCDW/L, 3e-13 gCDW/cell, 7e-16 L/cell for E. coli) and integrated with isotopomer balancing (13CFLUX2) into a unified net+exchange flux set, but the absolute numeric values reside in Supplementary Table 1, which is not present in this XML fulltext -- hence rate fields left null. Only relative/normalized fluxes appear in the text: oxPPP = 20% of glucose uptake; 2C into TCA = 24% of pyruvate. Growth rate not stated numerically in text. No fabricated numbers.

growth_records.snippet: we noted the measured doubling time of E. coli strain B/r (44 minutes), whose physiological and growth measurements were used to parameterize the model
growth_records.notes: This is a computational whole-cell/whole-colony modeling paper; its own reported doubling time (50.89 +/- 4.61 min) and per-cell glucose uptake distributions are SIMULATION outputs, not experimental measurements, so they are excluded. The single EXPERIMENTAL prokaryote datum is the cited measured doubling time of E. coli B/r = 44 min, taken from the model's parameterization reference (not measured in this study). Converted: doubling_time = 44 min = 44/60 = 0.7333 h; mu = ln2/td = 0.693/0.7333 = 0.945 /h. No experimental in-study uptake or secretion rates and no experimental medium composition for the B/r datum are reported here (medium_composition_stated:false; the 1 mM glucose M9 refers to the simulation environment). Temperature 37C assumed for E. coli B/r balanced growth (standard, not explicitly stated for the cited value).

growth_records.notes: mu = 0.081 +/- 0.002 h-1 reported (exponential regression, n=3). Curator computed doubling time: td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.081 = 8.56 h. growth_records.snippet: steady-state and time-course experimental data obtained from a unique E. coli strain (the model strain K-12 MG1655) grown under a unique reference condition (M9 minimal medium with glucose as sole carbon source, dilution rate = 0.1 h-1, tem
growth_records.notes: Kinetic-model paper. The reference growth condition is an aerobic glucose-limited chemostat: D=0.1 /h, 37C, pH 7.0, pO2>20%, M9 + glucose, E. coli K-12 MG1655. At steady state mu=D=0.1 /h (calculated); doubling_time=ln2/mu=0.693/0.1=6.93 h. Experimental validation data (growth rate, glucose and oxygen uptake rates, citrate-synthase/TCA fluxes, acetate) were taken from literature (refs 13,34-37; Ishii et al. 2007 Science 316:593) and shown as simulated-vs-experimental scatter in Figs 4-6; NO explicit numeric measured exchange-flux values appear in the readable text, so uptake/secretion rates left null (not invented). Model fluxes are internal (mM/s). M9 salt composition not itemised in text -> medium_composition_stated=false.

growth_records.cite: Golan, Ohad et al. Overall biomass yield on multiple nutrient sources. NPJ Systems Biology and Applications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41540-025-00497-y. [PMC11811147]
growth_records.snippet: we follow growth of E. coli until depletion of the initial nutrient source ... in M9 minimal medium with glucose, malate, or aspartate as sole carbon sources. These carbon sources were chosen as respiro-fermentative, strictly respiratory, a
growth_records.notes: Experimental prokaryote batch growth, E. coli NCM3722, M9 minimal + glucose, 37C, 350 rpm aerobic. Measured physiology is OVERALL BIOMASS YIELD (Yxs) = slope of produced biomass (ΔB = stationary biomass - inoculum biomass) vs initial glucose amount; reported ONLY graphically (Fig S1), no numeric Yxs in text. OD600 converted to dry weight using cited calibration 0.396 gDW/(L*OD) (from ref 18, Basan et al.-type conversion, NOT measured here). No numeric growth rate, uptake rate, or MFA reported; authors explicitly note the model 'is limited by its exclusion of dynamic data, such as metabolic flux rates and growth rates.' Acetate secretion measured (assay kit) but only as a relative slope, no absolute rate. M9 salt composition not enumerated in text. Carbon amount titrated 0-1.2 g/l; seed/pre-culture used 4 g/l glucose at 37C 350 rpm.

up.method: batch OD600 growth curve; no numeric uptake rate

yields.Yxs: overall biomass yield on malate = slope of produced biomass vs initial malate amount (Fig 1B, linear fit R2>0.9); numeric value not stated in text

growth_records.snippet: The produced biomass shows a good linear fit to the initial amount of the sole carbon source (Fig. 1B) and as such, can be described by ΔB = Y_X/D * N_D ... The slope of the linear fit is the overall biomass yield (fit parameter R2 > 0.9).
growth_records.notes: E. coli NCM3722, M9 minimal + malate (strictly respiratory C source), aerobic batch, 37C. Overall biomass yield reported graphically only (Fig 1B slope), no numeric Yxs, no growth rate, no uptake/secretion rate, no MFA in text. OD->DW via cited 0.396 gDW/(L*OD). M9 salts not enumerated.

growth_records.snippet: in M9 minimal medium with glucose, malate, or aspartate as sole carbon sources ... a respiro-fermentative, strictly respiratory, and a degradable biomass component. Data for glucose and aspartate experiments are shown in Fig. S1.

NOTE: paper also studies co-utilization (xylose or methionine as 'measured' nutrient on glucose/acetate/succinate base nutrients, and aspartate on glucose/acetate base) with diauxic two-phase growth on glucose->secreted acetate; all yields graphical, no numeric rates.

growth_records.strain: K-12 JM108 (proline auxotroph); carrying 12.0 kbp pUC-origin plasmid, 53% GC, kanR (no kanamycin added)
growth_records.medium: Chemically defined glucose-minimal batch + linear glucose feed (non-sulfate-limited control)

up.method: Linear feed 4.45 g glucose/h (=320 g/L * 13.91 mL/h); qGlc=feed/biomass; at feed start X=1.51 g -> 4.45/1.51=2.95 g/gDW/h /180.16 g/mol =16.4 mmol/gDW/h. Glucose stayed below detection limit, confirming qGlc=feed rate. Decreases to ~4% (~0.66 mmol/gDW/h) by 23 h due to constant feed / rising biomass.

growth_records.cite: Gotsmy et al. Sulfate limitation increases specific plasmid DNA yield and productivity in E. coli fed-batch processes. Microbial Cell Factories (2023). DOI:10.1186/s12934-023-02248-2. [PMC10685491]
growth_records.snippet: control 57.1 +/- 0.7 | 2528 +/- 68 | 81.5 +/- 2.2 | 510 | 44.2 +/- 0.9 | 12.0 pUC None JM108 Lin. fed-batch (Table 2, all pDNA conformations, t=31 h). Glucose ... remained below the detection limit, indicating that cells consistently mainta
growth_records.notes: Control (non-sulfate-limited) linear fed-batch, E. coli JM108, n=3. Growth non-constant (linear feed => continuously decreasing mu). Feed phase ~5 generations in 35 h -> avg mu=5*ln2/35=0.099 h-1; doubling=7.0 h (approximation; instantaneous mu declines). Biomass: batch end 1.51 g CDM in 0.5 L (3.0 g/L) -> final 44.2 g/L (Table 2). Glucose uptake FEED-DERIVED: total glucose 48.5 g CDM /Y(0.303 g/g)=160 g over 0.5 L feed =320 g/L; *13.91 mL/h =4.45 g/h; qGlc(t)=4.45/X; feed-start (X=1.51 g) =2.95 g/gDW/h=16.4 mmol/gDW/h (glucose MW 180.16); glucose below detection confirms uptake=feed. Y_biomass/glucose=0.303 g/g is the design coefficient stated by authors. pDNA is an INTRACELLULAR product (not excreted) -> reported in yields, not secretion; specific yield 57.1 mg/g at 31 h (=0.0571 g/g). No acetate/organic-acid secretion reported (aerobic, DO>30%).

growth_records.strain: K-12 JM108 (proline auxotroph); carrying 12.0 kbp pUC-origin plasmid, 53% GC, kanR (no kanamycin added)
growth_records.medium: Chemically defined glucose-minimal, sulfate-limited (SLIM); initial SO4 3.8 g/L, sulfate-free feed

up.units: mmol/gDW/h (feed-start value; drops to ~0.66 mmol/gDW/h at 23 h)
up.method: Same linear feed 4.45 g glucose/h; qGlc=feed/biomass; feed-start 2.95 g/gDW/h=16.4 mmol/gDW/h; authors state specific glucose uptake had dropped to 4% of initial (~0.66 mmol/gDW/h) at onset of sulfate starvation (23 h). Glucose below detection throughout, so uptake=feed.

growth_records.cite: Gotsmy et al. Sulfate limitation increases specific plasmid DNA yield and productivity in E. coli fed-batch processes. Microbial Cell Factories (2023). DOI:10.1186/s12934-023-02248-2. [PMC10685491]
growth_records.snippet: SO4 2- limited 73.9 +/- 4.6 | 2788 +/- 161 | 89.9 +/- 5.2 | 660 | 38.2 +/- 1.0 | 12.0 pUC None JM108 Lin. fed-batch ... specific pDNA yield rises ... reaching a maximum of 0.074 g g-1 after 31 h ... improvement of 29% compared to control (p
growth_records.notes: Sulfate-limited (SLIM) linear fed-batch, E. coli JM108, n=6. Growth arrests upon SO4 starvation (~23 h); before that same linear-feed growth as control (avg mu~0.099 h-1, doubling 7.0 h over growing feed phase; declining). Final biomass 38.2 g/L (Table 2; lower than control 44.2 because growth stops at 23 h + dilution). Glucose uptake feed-derived as in control record: feed-start 16.4 mmol/gDW/h, dropping to ~4% (~0.66 mmol/gDW/h) at 23 h. Specific pDNA yield 73.9 mg/g (=0.074 g/g) at 31 h, +29% vs control; volumetric productivity 89.9 mg/L/h (+10%); plasmid copy number +29% (660 vs 510); supercoiled fraction +3% -> +33% supercoiled specific yield. pDNA is intracellular product (yields, not secretion). Y_biomass/glucose=0.303 g/g design value.

growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (E. coli K12), fed-batch aerobic shake-flask cytidine fermentation. Initial glucose = 10 mL x 500 g/L / 100 mL = 50 g/L; two further 50 g/L-equivalent glucose pulses at 8 h and 24 h => fed-batch. Cytidine titer 1.58 +/- 0.03 g/L at 39 h (measured). Derived volumetric productivity = 1.58/39 = 0.0405 g/L/h (shown; NOT a specific mmol/gDW/h rate because no biomass/CDW reported). Acetate measured only as relative metabolomics abundance (this WT strain has the highest acetate; double mutant is 5.26-fold lower); no absolute acetate g/L or specific flux given. Growth followed as OD600 curve (Fig. 3A) but no numeric mu, doubling time, or CDW reported, so growth_rate/doubling_time null. pH monitored (declined during fermentation) but not controlled/set. LB base composition not enumerated in paper; only glucose supplement quantified, hence medium_composition_stated=false.

Distinct condition from the N-limited record because the limiting nutrient (and hence salt composition) differs.

growth_records.strain: JA122 (mutator/nonsense-suppressor; MutY Leu299*, RpoS Gln33*, RpoD Glu26*, glnX suppressor)
growth_records.cite: Kinnersley, Margie et al. Evolutionary dynamics and structural consequences of de novo beneficial mutations and mutant lineages arising in a constant environment. BMC Biol (2021). DOI:10.1186/s12915-021-00954-0. [PMC7863352]
growth_records.snippet: Chemostats (300 mL working volume) were run under aerobic conditions for 300-500 generations at constant temperature (30 C) and at constant dilution rate (D = 0.2 h-1). Under these conditions, steady-state population density is ~10^8 cells
growth_records.notes: Experimental evolution in glucose-limited aerobic chemostats. At steady state specific growth rate equals dilution rate: mu = D = 0.2 h-1 [calculated]. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.2 = 3.47 h. Glucose 0.0125% w/v = 0.125 g/L (limiting substrate; residual below detection). No measured qS or product secretion rates reported in main text (physiology/genomics focus). Davis Minimal Medium base composition not numerically stated (cited to Adams et al ref 36). Triplicate vessels share identical conditions => single condition record.

up.method: Biosen C-Line glucose analyzer; glucose uptake set as measured external efflux for 13C-MFA (absolute value in Supplementary Table S3, not in main-text XML)

sec.method: Ion-exchange chromatography with pH-buffered electroconductivity (Shimadzu HPLC); measured acetate efflux for 13C-MFA (absolute value in Supplementary Table S3, not in main-text XML)

mfa.units: % (all central-carbon fluxes normalized to specific glucose uptake rate = 100%)
mfa.note: Wild-type MG1655 reference; upper part of central carbon metabolism correlates with previously published data (ref 81). Absolute glucose uptake rate not given in main-text XML (figures give the flux map)
Wild-type reference level; the ppa-replacement strain shows a statistically significant 36% DECREASE relative to this WT value (see comparison record)

growth_records.notes: Table 2 (48 h). growth_records.snippet: ES02 showed limited glucose consumption and a higher xylose consumption rate than ES03 (4.73 vs. 2.23 mmol xyl g DCW-1 h-1), the strain ES02 was selected for further studies.
growth_records.notes: ES03 (ES02 Δcrr) preliminary evaluation at HALF carbon-source concentration (i.e. ~16.5 mM xylose): reported qsxyl=2.23 mmol xyl/gDCW/h (vs ES02 4.73 under the same preliminary half-carbon condition). µ not reported for ES03. This is the preliminary/supplementary dataset; barely consumed 7 mM xylose in 24 h. pH not stated.

growth_records.snippet: in the xylose-glucose mixture, the strain ES02/pCL_XylRQ31K outperformed ES02, increasing not only the qsxyl by 3.6-fold, from 2.55 to 9.16 mmol xyl g DCW-1 h-1, but also the µxyl by 2.7-fold, from 0.14 to 0.38 h-1.
growth_records.notes: ES02/pCL_XylR^Q31K on xylose-glucose mixture: reported µxyl=0.38 h-1 (2.7-fold over ES02 0.14); qsxyl=9.16 mmol xyl/gDCW/h (3.6-fold over ES02 2.55). Xylose preferentially consumed; glucose only after xylose depletion (~8-12 mM in 18 h, no numeric qs_glc). td=ln2/0.38=1.82 h. pH not stated.

growth_records.snippet: The comparison between strain ES04 and the previous strain ES02 indicates no significant change... in the µxyl (0.37 vs. 0.35 h-1) or the qsxyl (10.51 vs. 11.26 mmol xyl gDCW-1 h-1) when cultured with xylose... an increase in the maximum bi
growth_records.notes: ES04 (chromosomal xylR C91A / XylR Q31K) on 33 mM xylose: reported µxyl=0.37 h-1; qsxyl=11.26 mmol xyl/gDCW/h (comparison pair 10.51 vs 11.26 is ES02/pCL_XylRQ31K vs ES04). Xmax=0.85 gDCW/L. µxyl of ES04 equals µglc of WT BL21(DE3). td=ln2/0.37=1.87 h. Approx Yxs derived: Xmax 0.85 gDCW/L / (33 mM xylose x 0.15013 g/mmol = 4.954 g/L) = 0.172 gDCW/g xylose (assumes near-complete consumption and negligible inoculum biomass; approximate). pH not stated.

up.method: model tuning constraint (maximal glucose uptake set to 10.5 mmol gCDW-1 h-1 to match observations), not a directly reported measurement
model constraint (O2 uptake limited to max 15 mmol gCDW-1 h-1); not a direct measurement

sec.method: model acetate excretion tuned to match experimental observations (reported as 2.2 mM gCDW-1 h-1, i.e. mmol gCDW-1 h-1); acetate was the only appreciably excreted by-product observed in medium NMR analysis

growth_records.cite: Karlsen E et al. A study of a diauxic growth experiment using an expanded dynamic flux balance framework. PLOS ONE (2023). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0280077. [PMC9821518]
growth_records.method: reported (measured from exponential-phase OD600; mu=0.66 h-1 stated). Doubling time calculated: ln2/0.66=0.693/0.66=1.05 h
growth_records.snippet: During the exponential growth phases, growth rates were measured to 0.66 h-1, while the terminal biomass values were measured to 8.2 gCDW L-1.
growth_records.notes: Experimental measured values (priority): mu=0.66 h-1 (exponential phase), terminal biomass 8.2 gCDW/L. Doubling time computed mu=ln2/td => td=ln2/0.66=1.05 h. Glucose (10.5) and O2 (15) uptake and acetate (2.2) excretion are decFBA model constraints tuned to reproduce the fermentation, NOT direct rate measurements, hence is_experimental=false; raw fermentor/off-gas data are in S4 File. Medium is glucose minimal M9 (recipe in S1 File, not in main text) => medium_composition_stated=false. Acetate excretion was experimentally observed as the sole appreciable by-product but its measured rate not tabulated in text. pH held >=7 by NaOH addition; 37 C; 40% DO.

up.method: HPLC; consumed sequentially (diauxic) before xylose; full 15 g/L glucose + 8 g/L xylose mixture consumed in ~8.5 h

sec.method: no products observed in aerobic batch fermentation

growth_records.method: not reported (OD600 correlated to dry cell mass; no numeric mu given)
growth_records.snippet: we observed diauxic growth... glucose and xylose were consumed sequentially, and... the complete consumption of this mixture required about 8.5 hours.
growth_records.notes: Aerobic diauxic control (Fig 1). Sugars consumed sequentially over ~8.5 h; no per-substrate specific rate or growth rate reported for aerobic phase. Avg volumetric consumption of total sugar = (15+8) g/L / 8.5 h = 2.7 g/L/h but split glucose-then-xylose not separable; left as null. No fermentation products aerobically.

growth_records.cite: Mark A Eiteman et al. A co-fermentation strategy to consume sugar mixtures effectively. Journal of Biological Engineering (2008). DOI:10.1186/1754-1611-2-3. [PMC2266900]
growth_records.snippet: For this co-culture, glucose was consumed in 7.5 h, and xylose was simultaneously consumed in 7.0 h... final OD of this mixed culture was about 25, identical to the sum of the ODs...
growth_records.notes: Aerobic co-culture (Fig 3). Both sugars consumed simultaneously/independently; volumetric rates = delta-S/time. Final OD ~25 = sum of the two single-strain ODs (10+15), indicating additive independent growth. ~15% faster than single-organism diauxic process.

up.method: substrate-limited: vessel glucose held at 0; uptake = exponentially increasing feed rate (mu 0.1/h), consumed by ALS1008 fraction
substrate-limited: vessel xylose held at 0; uptake = feed rate, consumed by ZSC113 fraction

growth_records.cite: Mark A Eiteman et al. A co-fermentation strategy to consume sugar mixtures effectively. Journal of Biological Engineering (2008). DOI:10.1186/1754-1611-2-3. [PMC2266900]
growth_records.method: reported (nominal set-point of exponential feed, mu=0.1/h); doubling_time = ln2/0.1 = 6.93 h (calculated)
growth_records.snippet: During the entire fed-batch process, the xylose and glucose concentrations in the fermenter remained at zero... the distribution of the microbial population responded in unison with the shift in substrate concentrations.
growth_records.notes: Aerobic fed-batch (Fig 4). mu set at 0.1/h via exponential feed (far below max growth rate). Population self-adjusted: ZSC113 fraction 35% (20:30) ->50% (30:30) ->42% (30:60) ->32% (20:60), tracking xylose:glucose feed ratio. Sugars held at zero => each substrate individually limiting. Per-strain specific uptake not tabulated numerically.

up.method: reported specific rate 630 mg/gh; /180.16 g/mol = 3.50 mmol/gDW/h (10 g/L consumed in 2.5 h = 4 g/L/h at OD 21)
reported initial specific rate 310 mg/gh; /150.13 = 2.07 mmol/gDW/h (2 g/L/h); later dropped to <150 mg/gh (<1.0 mmol/gDW/h) after 4 h

sec.method: mixed-acid products measured (formate, lactate, succinate, acetate, ethanol); formate highest-yield product; per-substrate yield not isolated for WT mixed feed

growth_records.cite: Mark A Eiteman et al. A co-fermentation strategy to consume sugar mixtures effectively. Journal of Biological Engineering (2008). DOI:10.1186/1754-1611-2-3. [PMC2266900]
growth_records.snippet: 10 g/L of glucose was consumed in about 2.5 h (4 g/Lh), equivalent to a specific glucose consumption rate of 630 mg/gh. Initially xylose was consumed at a rate of 310 mg/gh (2 g/Lh)... after 4 h... decreased to less than 150 mg/gh.
growth_records.notes: Anaerobic mixed-acid (Fig 5), single WT strain OD 21. qGlc 630 mg/gh -> 630/180.16 = 3.497 mmol/gDW/h. qXyl 310 mg/gh -> 310/150.13 = 2.065 mmol/gDW/h initial, falling <150 mg/gh (<1.0 mmol/gDW/h) after 4 h (attributed to acetate inhibition of xylose uptake). Products qualitatively measured; formate highest, lactate lowest.

up.method: reported specific rate 500 mg/gh; /150.13 = 3.33 mmol/gDW/h; constant 1.4 g/L/h at OD 9.5

growth_records.snippet: xylose was consumed at a constant rate of 1.4 g/Lh during the anaerobic phase... present only at an OD of 9.5, so that on a specific basis the xylose consumption rate was 500 mg/gh... succinate yield from xylose using ZSC113 was 0.48 mol/mo
growth_records.notes: Anaerobic (Fig 6a). qXyl 500 mg/gh -> 500/150.13 = 3.331 mmol/gDW/h; greater than WT xylose rate. Glucose untouched. Product yields on xylose: succinate 0.48, formate 1.58 mol/mol.

up.method: reported specific rate ~770 mg/gh; /180.16 = 4.27 mmol/gDW/h; constant 3 g/L/h

growth_records.notes: Anaerobic (Fig 6b). qGlc 770 mg/gh -> 770/180.16 = 4.274 mmol/gDW/h; greater than WT glucose rate. Xylose untouched. Product yields on glucose: succinate 0.30, formate 1.24 mol/mol.

growth_records.cite: Mark A Eiteman et al. A co-fermentation strategy to consume sugar mixtures effectively. Journal of Biological Engineering (2008). DOI:10.1186/1754-1611-2-3. [PMC2266900]
growth_records.snippet: the xylose consumption rate was therefore about 475 mg/gh, while the glucose consumption rate was about 1300 mg/gh... the two-strain process is much faster than an otherwise identical single-strain process.
growth_records.notes: Anaerobic co-culture (Fig 7). Specific rates depend on ESTIMATED per-strain ODs (ZSC113 ~13, ALS1008 ~9) inferred from single-substrate aerobic runs, not measured directly here -> confidence medium. qXyl 475 mg/gh = 3.164 mmol/gDW/h; qGlc 1300 mg/gh = 7.216 mmol/gDW/h. Mixture yields: succinate 0.41, formate 1.46 mol/mol.

growth_records.snippet: For the minimal media no limitation batch cultures, NCM3722 cells were grown at 37 C in 40 mM MOPS media with glucose (0.4%), ammonium (9.5 mM) and phosphate (1.32 mM)... grown up to an OD ~0.4
growth_records.notes: Growth rate calculated from reported 42-min doubling time: mu = ln2/(42/60 h) = 0.693/0.700 = 0.990 h^-1; doubling_time_h = 0.70. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates reported (protein-turnover study). pH taken as MOPS-buffered 7.2 (chemostat pH held 7.2+/-0.1; batch pH not separately stated). Glucose 0.4% w/v = 4 g/L ~= 22.2 mM.

growth_records.cite: Meera Gupta et al. Global protein turnover quantification in Escherichia coli reveals cytoplasmic recycling under nitrogen limitation. Nature Communications (2024). DOI:10.1038/s41467-024-49920-8. [PMC11246515]
growth_records.snippet: E. coli strain NCM3722 was grown in continuous culture chemostats at 37 C... For C- and N-limiting media, glucose and ammonium concentrations were reduced by fivefold (0.08% and 1.9 mM, respectively).
growth_records.notes: Chemostat at steady state: mu = D = ln2/doubling_time = ln2/3 h = 0.231 h^-1. Glucose reduced fivefold (0.08% w/v = 0.8 g/L) to impose carbon limitation. No uptake/secretion rates reported.

growth_records.strain: LE392 (supE44 supF58 hsdR514 galK2 galT22 metB1 trpR55 lacY1); plasmid-free host

yields.Yxs: 0.5 g biomass/g glucose (measured in batch, non-transformed and plasmid-bearing LE392)

growth_records.cite: Popov, Mladen et al. Effects of a recombinant gene expression on ColE1-like plasmid segregation in Escherichia coli. BMC Biotechnology (2011). DOI:10.1186/1472-6750-11-18. [PMC3061898]
growth_records.method: reported (mu_max experimentally estimated from batch growth kinetics); doubling time calculated td=ln2/0.66=1.05 h
growth_records.snippet: a parameter value of 0.66 h-1 was estimated from the growth kinetics of E. coli LE392 cells cultivated in minimal (M9) medium supplemented with glucose in a batch reactor ... By batch cultivations ... a yield factor Yx/s = 0.5 g biomass/g g
growth_records.notes: mu_max=0.66/h measured in M9+glucose batch (Table 3, Results). Yx/s=0.5 g/g measured in batch (both non-transformed and plasmid-bearing LE392). Doubling time calculated td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.66=1.05 h. Glucose uptake DERIVED: qS=mu/Yxs=0.66/0.5=1.32 g glucose/gDW/h; converted to molar 1.32/180.16*1000=7.33 mmol/gDW/h (glucose MW 180.16). Substrate for product/endogenous metabolism was neglected in the paper's model. No secreted products quantified (aerobic glucose-limited).
growth_records.conf: high for mu_max & Yxs (both experimental); medium for derived glucose uptake

growth_records.strain: LE392 transformed with pBR322-based hIFNgamma plasmids (pP1-(SD)-hIFNg, pP1-(4SD)-hIFNg, pP1-(dSD)-hIFNg, pdP1-(dSD)-hIFNg); Np=41/24/30/22 respectively

yields.Yxs: 0.5 g biomass/g glucose (batch value applied; paper notes chemostat Yx/s decreases with increasing D per Seo & Bailey, so chemostat Yxs likely <0.5)

growth_records.cite: Popov, Mladen et al. Effects of a recombinant gene expression on ColE1-like plasmid segregation in Escherichia coli. BMC Biotechnology (2011). DOI:10.1186/1472-6750-11-18. [PMC3061898]
growth_records.method: calculated: steady-state chemostat mu=D=0.3/h; doubling td=ln2/0.3=2.31 h
growth_records.snippet: Feeding of chemostat cultivations was initiated after the initial batch phase ... at a constant dilution rate of 0.3 h-1 ... M9 medium containing 1.96 g/L glucose (without antibiotic) ... pH 7.0 ... 37 C and 600 rpm ... pO2 controlled at 80
growth_records.notes: Steady-state chemostat: mu=D=0.3/h (D from Table 3). Doubling td=ln2/0.3=2.31 h. Glucose uptake DERIVED with batch Yxs=0.5: qS=mu/Yxs=0.3/0.5=0.6 g/gDW/h = 3.33 mmol/gDW/h (glucose MW 180.16). CAVEAT: paper explicitly states (Seo & Bailey ref 42) that Yx/s decreases with increasing dilution rate, so true chemostat qS at D=0.3 is likely higher than 3.33. Feed glucose sF=1.96 g/L; residual glucose s near-zero (glucose-limited, Ks=0.005 g/L assumed). Plasmid copy numbers measured by QPCR 20 h after feed start: 41 (pP1-SD), 24 (pP1-4SD), 22 (pdP1-dSD), 30 (pP1-dSD). hIFNg is intracellular, non-secreted; no external product secretion.
growth_records.conf: high for mu=D and conditions; low-medium for derived glucose uptake (batch Yxs applied to chemostat)

growth_records.cite: Cermak, Nathan et al. High-throughput measurement of single-cell growth rates using serial microfluidic mass sensor arrays. Nature Biotechnology (2016). DOI:10.1038/nbt.3666. [PMC5064867]
growth_records.snippet: In rich media (LB) at 37 C, we found that the average growth rate of individual bacteria was 2.14 +/- 0.02/h (mean +/- standard error, 19.4 minute doubling time) with a coefficient of variation of 7.4% excluding four outlying small cells (F
growth_records.notes: Single-cell exponential growth rate (mass accumulation rate per unit mass) measured directly by serial SMR array. mu = 2.14 +/- 0.02 /h reported. Doubling time reported as 19.4 min = 0.323 h; check ln2/2.14 = 0.3239 h = 19.4 min (consistent). CV of growth rate 7.4% (four outlying small cells excluded). No substrate-uptake, product-secretion, MFA flux, or yield data reported (method/instrumentation paper). E. coli grown overnight in LB broth (Miller, Difco) at 37C, diluted 10,000-fold in LB + 0.2% Tween-80 (Tween added to prevent sticking to device walls), incubated 1.5-2.5 h at 37C before loading. Base LB Miller composition not stated in paper -> medium_composition_stated false. pH not stated.
